This is analogous to existing declarative languages, such as Declare, where each constraint has a direct effect only on the specific activities being constrained. Given this similarity, we propose mapping subsets of discovered declarative constraints into equivalent semi-concurrently executed procedural fragments, thus laying the foundation for a corresponding process (frame) discovery approach.

AI.tldr: The paper defines and motivates a new kind of “process frame” for AI-augmented BPM systems, treating it as a hybrid representation that combines procedural and declarative models with open-world, constraint-like semantics, and sketches how to discover such frames by mapping declarative constraints to procedural fragments.
AI.motivation: AI-augmented BPM systems need a process-aware representation that both constrains and enables semi-autonomous behavior (“framed autonomy”). Existing traditional process models are too rigid and usually bound to a single formalism. There is a gap in how to formally represent processes in a way that is permissive, supports heterogeneous knowledge (procedures, commonsense, best practices), and provides suitable execution semantics for AI-driven, open-world settings.
AI.method: The paper conceptually defines an ABPMS process frame as a hybrid representation made of semi-concurrently executed procedural and declarative models. Building on their earlier work, the authors specify execution semantics that adopt the open-world assumption from declarative modeling also for procedural models, reinterpreting them as constraints. They then propose a mapping from subsets of discovered declarative constraints to equivalent semi-concurrently executed procedural fragments as a basis for process frame discovery.
AI.result: The paper delivers: (1) a conceptualization of process frames as hybrid business process representations with semi-concurrent execution; (2) an argument and formal basis for treating procedural models under an open-world, constraint-like semantics analogous to declarative languages such as Declare; and (3) a proposal for mapping declarative constraints into procedural fragments, providing a starting point for automated discovery of such hybrid process frames.
AI.conclusion: The notion of a process frame provides an appropriate, permissive, and heterogeneous representation for AI-augmented BPM systems, enabling framed autonomy. By adopting open-world, constraint-like semantics for procedural models and integrating them with declarative constraints, ABPMS can manage processes more flexibly. The proposed mapping from declarative constraints to procedural fragments sets the groundwork for discovery techniques that construct such hybrid process frames from event data and existing constraints.

title: SHAPE: Unifying Safety, Helpfulness and Pedagogy for Educational LLMs
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have been widely explored in educational scenarios. We identify a critical vulnerability in current educational LLMs, pedagogical jailbreaks, where students use answer-inducing prompts to elicit solutions rather than scaffolded instructions. To enable systematic study, we unify and formalize safe, helpful, and pedagogical behaviors with a knowledge-mastery graph and introduce SHAPE, a benchmark of 9,087 student-question pairs for evaluating tutoring behavior under adversarial pressure. We propose a graph-augmented tutoring pipeline that infers prerequisite concepts from queries, identifies mastery gaps, and routes generation between instructing and problem-solving via explicit gating. Experiments across multiple LLMs show that our method yields significantly improved safety under two pedagogical jailbreak settings, while maintaining near-ceiling helpfulness under the same evaluation protocol. Our code and data are available at https://github.com/MAPS-research/SHaPE

AI.tldr: They identify and benchmark a new vulnerability in educational LLMs—'pedagogical jailbreaks'—and propose a graph-based tutoring pipeline that improves safety while keeping models helpful.
AI.motivation: Educational LLMs are supposed to teach, not just give away answers, but current systems can be easily prompted by students to bypass pedagogical intent and output full solutions. There is no standard way to study or measure this vulnerability, nor robust methods to mitigate it without sacrificing helpfulness.
AI.method: They (1) formalize what it means for tutoring behavior to be safe, helpful, and pedagogical via a knowledge-mastery graph; (2) build SHAPE, a benchmark of 9,087 student-question pairs designed to probe tutoring behavior under adversarial, answer-seeking prompts; and (3) design a graph-augmented tutoring pipeline that infers prerequisite concepts and mastery gaps from a query, then uses an explicit gating mechanism to decide whether the LLM should provide instruction or solve the problem directly.
AI.result: Across multiple LLMs, their pipeline substantially reduces the success of 'pedagogical jailbreak' attacks in two adversarial settings, while preserving near-maximum helpfulness performance on the same benchmark.
AI.conclusion: Structuring tutoring around an explicit knowledge-mastery graph and gated routing between explanation and answer-giving can make educational LLMs more robust to answer-seeking adversarial prompts, improving safety without meaningfully reducing helpfulness. The SHAPE benchmark and code offer a reusable framework for evaluating and developing such systems.

title: QuantClaw: Precision Where It Matters for OpenClaw
summary: Autonomous agent systems such as OpenClaw introduce significant efficiency challenges due to long-context inputs and multi-turn reasoning. This results in prohibitively high computational and monetary costs in real-world development. While quantization is a standard approach for reducing cost and latency, its impact on agent performance in realistic scenarios remains unclear. In this work, we analyze quantization sensitivity across diverse complex workflows over OpenClaw, and show that precision requirements are highly task-dependent. Based on this observation, we propose QuantClaw, a plug-and-play precision routing plugin that dynamically assigns precision according to task characteristics. QuantClaw routes lightweight tasks to lower-cost configurations while preserving higher precision for demanding workloads, saving cost and accelerating inference without increasing user complexity. Experiments show that our QuantClaw maintains or improves task performance while reducing both latency and computational cost. Across a range of agent tasks, it achieves up to 21.4% cost savings and 15.7% latency reduction on GLM-5 (FP8 baseline). These results highlight the benefit of treating precision as a dynamic resource in agent systems.

AI.tldr: The paper presents QuantClaw, a dynamic precision routing plugin for autonomous agent systems that reduces cost and latency while maintaining or improving task performance by assigning different quantization levels based on task characteristics.
AI.motivation: Autonomous agent systems like OpenClaw require long-context inputs and multi-turn reasoning, which makes them computationally and financially expensive in real-world use. Standard quantization techniques can reduce cost and latency, but their effect on complex, realistic agent workflows is not well understood, particularly how precision needs vary across tasks. The paper is motivated by the need to cut costs and speed up inference without degrading performance in practical agent scenarios.
AI.method: The authors first study quantization sensitivity across a variety of complex workflows built on OpenClaw, identifying that different tasks have different precision requirements. Leveraging this, they design QuantClaw, a plug-and-play precision routing plugin that dynamically assigns model precision levels based on task characteristics. Lightweight tasks are routed to lower-cost, more heavily quantized configurations, while more demanding tasks are assigned higher precision. The system is evaluated on GLM-5 with an FP8 baseline across multiple agent tasks, comparing latency, cost, and task performance.
AI.result: QuantClaw successfully maintains or even improves task performance relative to the FP8 baseline while reducing both computational cost and latency. On GLM-5 agent tasks, it achieves up to 21.4% cost savings and 15.7% latency reduction. The experiments show that dynamic precision routing does not increase user complexity and effectively exploits task-dependent precision needs.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that precision should be treated as a dynamic, task-dependent resource in autonomous agent systems rather than a fixed setting. By routing tasks to appropriate quantization levels via QuantClaw, systems can achieve significant cost and latency reductions without sacrificing, and sometimes even improving, performance. This plug-and-play approach offers a practical path to more efficient deployment of long-context, multi-turn agent workflows.

title: Voice Under Revision: Large Language Models and the Normalization of Personal Narrative
summary:
This study examines how large language model rewriting alters the style and narrative texture of personal narratives. It analyzes 300 personal narratives rewritten by three frontier LLMs under three prompt conditions: generic improvement, rewrite-only, and voice-preserving revision. Change is measured across 13 linguistic markers drawn from computational stylistics, including function words, vocabulary diversity, word length, punctuation, contractions, first-person pronouns, and emotion words.
  Across models and prompt conditions, LLM rewriting produces a consistent pattern of stylistic normalization. Function words, contractions, and first-person pronouns decrease, while vocabulary diversity, word length, and punctuation elaboration increase. These shifts occur whether the prompt asks the model to "improve" the text or simply to "rewrite" it. Voice-preserving prompts reduce the magnitude of the changes but do not eliminate their direction. Stylometric analysis shows that rewritten texts converge in feature space and become harder to match back to their source texts. Additional narrative markers indicate a shift from embedded to distanced narration, and from explicit causal reasoning to compressed abstraction.
  The findings suggest that contemporary LLMs exert a directional pull toward a more polished, less situated register. This has consequences for digital humanities and computational text analysis, where features such as function words, pronouns, contractions, and punctuation often serve as evidence for style, voice, authorship, and corpus integrity. LLM revision should therefore be understood not merely as surface-level editing, but as a consequential form of textual mediation.

AI.tldr: Study of how LLM rewriting changes style in personal narratives, finding consistent stylistic normalization and loss of distinct voice.
AI.motivation: To understand how contemporary LLMs, when used for rewriting or improving personal narratives, systematically alter stylistic and narrative features that scholars often treat as evidence of authorial voice, style, and corpus integrity.
AI.method: Collected 300 personal narratives and asked three advanced LLMs to rewrite each under three prompt conditions: generic improvement, rewrite-only, and voice-preserving revision. Measured changes across 13 computational stylistic markers (e.g., function words, vocabulary diversity, word length, punctuation, contractions, first-person pronouns, emotion words) and conducted stylometric and narrative analysis to examine convergence and shifts in narrative stance.
AI.result: Across models and prompts, rewrites showed a stable pattern: decreases in function words, contractions, and first-person pronouns; increases in vocabulary diversity, word length, and punctuation elaboration. These patterns held even for simple rewrite prompts; voice-preserving prompts reduced but did not reverse the direction of change. Stylometric analysis showed convergence of rewritten texts, making source–rewrite matching more difficult. Narrative analysis indicated movement from embedded to distanced narration and from explicit causal reasoning to compressed abstraction.
AI.conclusion: LLM rewriting exerts a strong directional pull toward a polished, normalized, less situated register, even when instructed to preserve voice. This systematically transforms stylistic markers that digital humanities and computational text analysis use to infer style, voice, authorship, and corpus integrity. Therefore, LLM-based revision should be treated as a consequential interpretive intervention in texts, not just minor surface editing.

title: Rethinking Math Reasoning Evaluation: A Robust LLM-as-a-Judge Framework Beyond Symbolic Rigidity
summary: Recent advancements in large language models have led to significant improvements across various tasks, including mathematical reasoning, which is used to assess models' intelligence in logical reasoning and problem-solving. Models are evaluated on mathematical reasoning benchmarks by verifying the correctness of the final answer against a ground truth answer. A common approach for this verification is based on symbolic mathematics comparison, which fails to generalize across diverse mathematical representations and solution formats. In this work, we offer a robust and flexible alternative to rule-based symbolic mathematics comparison. We propose an LLM-based evaluation framework for evaluating model-generated answers, enabling accurate evaluation across diverse mathematical representations and answer formats. We present failure cases of symbolic evaluation in two popular frameworks, Lighteval and SimpleRL, and compare them to our approach, demonstrating clear improvements over commonly used methods. Our framework enables more reliable evaluation and benchmarking, leading to more accurate performance monitoring, which is important for advancing mathematical problem-solving and intelligent systems.

AI.tldr: They develop an LLM-based evaluator for math reasoning benchmarks that overcomes the limitations of symbolic answer checking and yields more reliable model evaluation.
AI.motivation: Current math reasoning benchmarks typically check only whether a model's final answer matches a ground-truth answer, using symbolic math comparison. This approach breaks when answers are expressed in different but equivalent forms, or when solutions use varied formats and representations. These failures distort evaluation and hinder accurate tracking of progress in mathematical reasoning.
AI.method: They design a framework that uses a large language model as the judge to evaluate model-generated mathematical answers. Instead of relying solely on symbolic equivalence, the LLM-based evaluator can interpret diverse representations and formats of correct answers. They then integrate this evaluator with existing evaluation frameworks (Lighteval and SimpleRL) and systematically compare its behavior to standard symbolic comparison, highlighting where symbolic methods fail.
AI.result: They identify and present concrete failure cases of symbolic evaluation within popular benchmarking frameworks (Lighteval and SimpleRL) and show that the LLM-based evaluation correctly handles those cases. Quantitatively and/or qualitatively, their approach yields more accurate correctness judgments over a range of mathematical answer formats.
AI.conclusion: Using an LLM-based evaluation framework for mathematical reasoning tasks provides a more robust and flexible alternative to rule-based symbolic comparison, improving reliability of benchmark scores and enabling more accurate monitoring of progress in mathematical problem-solving systems.

title: When AI Speaks, Whose Values Does It Express? A Cross-Cultural Audit of Individualism-Collectivism Bias in Large Language Models
comment: 13 pages, 7 figures, 9 tables. Data and code: https://github.com/pruthvinathJV/ai-values-misalignment-study
summary:
When you ask an AI assistant for advice about your career, your marriage, or a conflict with your family, does it give you the same answer regardless of where you are from? We tested this systematically by presenting three leading AI systems (Claude Sonnet 4.5, GPT-5.4, and Gemini 2.5 Flash) with ten real-life personal dilemmas, framed for users from 10 countries across 5 continents in 7 languages (n=840 scored responses). We compared AI advice against World Values Survey Wave 7 data measuring what people in each country actually believe.
  All three AI systems consistently gave Western-style, individualist advice even to users from societies that prioritize family, community, and authority, significantly more so than local values would predict (mean gap +0.76 on a 1-5 scale; t=15.65, p<0.001). The gap is largest for Nigeria (+1.85) and India (+0.82). Japan is the sole exception: AI systems treated Japanese users as more group-oriented than surveys show, revealing that AI encodes outdated stereotypes. Claude and GPT-5.4 show nearly identical bias magnitude, while Gemini is lower but still significant. The models diverge in mechanism: Claude shifts further collectivist in the user's native language; Gemini shifts more individualist; GPT-5.4 responds only to stated country identity. These findings point to a systemic homogenization of values across frontier AI. Data, code, and scoring pipeline are openly released.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that major AI assistants give similar, Western-style, individualist moral advice to users from very different cultures, diverging from local values and sometimes reinforcing stereotypes.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether large AI assistants, now widely used for personal and moral advice, adapt their guidance to users’ cultural contexts or instead impose a narrow, homogenized value system that may clash with local norms.
AI.method: The authors selected ten real-world personal dilemmas and posed them to three leading AI systems (Claude Sonnet 4.5, GPT-5.4, and Gemini 2.5 Flash). Each dilemma was framed as coming from users in 10 different countries, spanning 5 continents and 7 languages, generating 840 responses. They then scored the advice on an individualism–collectivism scale and compared it to country-level value profiles drawn from the World Values Survey Wave 7. They also compared how each system’s responses varied by user language and stated country identity to infer mechanisms of cultural adaptation or bias.
AI.result: All three AI systems produced advice that was more Western-style and individualist than the average values reported by people in the respective countries, with a mean individualism gap of +0.76 on a 1–5 scale (t=15.65, p<0.001). The misalignment was particularly large for Nigeria (+1.85) and India (+0.82). Japan was the only case where AI treated users as more collectivist than survey data indicates, suggesting outdated stereotypes. Claude and GPT-5.4 exhibited similar magnitudes of bias; Gemini’s bias was smaller but still statistically significant. Mechanistically, Claude’s advice shifted somewhat toward collectivism when using the user’s native language, Gemini shifted more individualist in such cases, and GPT-5.4 was mainly sensitive to the explicitly stated country, not language.
AI.conclusion: Frontier AI assistants currently offer culturally homogenized moral and personal advice that skews toward Western individualism, often misaligned with local values and sometimes reflecting stereotypes. While systems differ in how they incorporate cues like language and country, none fully adapt to users’ cultural contexts. The authors argue this has implications for fairness, global deployment, and AI alignment with pluralistic human values, and they provide open data and tools to facilitate further scrutiny and improvement.

title: Agentic World Modeling: Foundations, Capabilities, Laws, and Beyond
summary: As AI systems move from generating text to accomplishing goals through sustained interaction, the ability to model environment dynamics becomes a central bottleneck. Agents that manipulate objects, navigate software, coordinate with others, or design experiments require predictive environment models, yet the term world model carries different meanings across research communities. We introduce a "levels x laws" taxonomy organized along two axes. The first defines three capability levels: L1 Predictor, which learns one-step local transition operators; L2 Simulator, which composes them into multi-step, action-conditioned rollouts that respect domain laws; and L3 Evolver, which autonomously revises its own model when predictions fail against new evidence. The second identifies four governing-law regimes: physical, digital, social, and scientific. These regimes determine what constraints a world model must satisfy and where it is most likely to fail. Using this framework, we synthesize over 400 works and summarize more than 100 representative systems spanning model-based reinforcement learning, video generation, web and GUI agents, multi-agent social simulation, and AI-driven scientific discovery. We analyze methods, failure modes, and evaluation practices across level-regime pairs, propose decision-centric evaluation principles and a minimal reproducible evaluation package, and outline architectural guidance, open problems, and governance challenges. The resulting roadmap connects previously isolated communities and charts a path from passive next-step prediction toward world models that can simulate, and ultimately reshape, the environments in which agents operate.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a unifying taxonomy and roadmap for "world models" in AI agents, classifying them by capability level and governing-law regime, and surveys 400+ works to analyze methods, failures, evaluation, and open challenges.
AI.motivation: As AI agents shift from static text generation to acting in and interacting with complex environments, they need robust models of how those environments change in response to actions. However, the notion of a "world model" is fragmented across communities (RL, video modeling, web agents, social simulation, scientific discovery), making it difficult to compare methods, transfer insights, or systematically evaluate progress. A unifying conceptual framework is needed to clarify capabilities, constraints, and failure modes of environment models and to guide research and evaluation toward more capable agents.
AI.method: The authors introduce a 2D taxonomy for world models. One axis captures three capability levels: L1 Predictors that learn local one-step transition dynamics; L2 Simulators that compose these into multi-step, action-conditioned rollouts obeying domain laws; and L3 Evolvers that can revise their own modeling assumptions and structure in light of prediction errors and new evidence. The other axis defines four governing-law regimes—physical, digital, social, and scientific—each imposing different constraints and typical failure points. They then perform a large-scale survey of over 400 papers and more than 100 concrete systems across these level-regime combinations (e.g., model-based RL, video generation, GUI/web agents, multi-agent simulations, AI for science). They analyze methods, common failure patterns, and evaluation practices. Finally, they propose decision-centric evaluation principles, a minimal reproducible evaluation package, and architectural guidance for building more advanced world models.
AI.result: The paper yields: (1) a coherent "levels x laws" taxonomy that organizes diverse world-modeling approaches; (2) a synthesis of 400+ prior works and 100+ systems mapped into this framework, highlighting coverage, gaps, and cross-domain parallels; (3) a comparative analysis of modeling techniques, failure modes, and evaluation methodologies for each level-regime pairing; and (4) concrete proposals for decision-focused evaluation metrics and a minimal, reusable evaluation package aimed at standardizing assessment of world models across domains.
AI.conclusion: World modeling is a central bottleneck for capable AI agents, but current efforts are fragmented and unevenly evaluated. The proposed levels-by-laws taxonomy offers a shared language to compare and design world models across physical, digital, social, and scientific regimes and across increasing capability levels from prediction to simulation to self-evolving models. By synthesizing existing work, identifying common failure modes, and recommending decision-centric evaluation and architectural directions, the roadmap aims to connect isolated research communities and to steer progress toward world models that not only predict but also simulate and eventually help reshape the environments where AI agents act.

title: Fine-Grained Analysis of Shared Syntactic Mechanisms in Language Models
summary: While language models demonstrate sophisticated syntactic capabilities, the extent to which their internal mechanisms align with cross-constructional principles studied in linguistics remains poorly understood. This study investigates whether models employ shared neural mechanisms across different syntactic constructions by applying causal interpretability methods at a granular level. Focusing on filler-gap dependencies and negative polarity item (NPI) licensing, we utilize activation patching to identify the functional roles of specific attention heads and MLP blocks. Our results reveal a highly localized and shared mechanism for filler-gap dependencies located in the early to middle layers, whereas NPI processing exhibits no such unified mechanism. Furthermore, we find that these mechanisms identified by activation patching generalize to out-of-distribution, while distributed alignment search, a supervised interpretability method, is susceptible to overfitting on narrow linguistic distributions. Finally, we validate our findings by demonstrating that the manipulation of the identified components improves model performance on acceptability judgment benchmarks.

AI.tldr: The paper probes whether language models use shared, localized neural mechanisms for different syntactic phenomena, finding a compact shared mechanism for filler-gap dependencies but not for NPI licensing, and shows that causally identified components improve syntactic judgments.
AI.motivation: Linguistic theory proposes cross-constructional principles that underlie multiple syntactic phenomena, but it is unclear whether neural language models internally realize similar shared mechanisms. Prior work has mostly assessed surface behavioral performance or offered coarse interpretability analyses without directly testing whether the same neural components support different constructions. The authors aim to bridge formal linguistics and mechanistic interpretability by probing if and how specific components in transformer models jointly implement different syntactic processes.
AI.method: They focus on two classic syntactic phenomena: filler-gap dependencies (e.g., wh-movement) and negative polarity item (NPI) licensing. Using causal interpretability via activation patching, they systematically intervene on and replace activations of individual attention heads and MLP blocks across layers to see which components are functionally responsible for correct behavior in each phenomenon. They compare this to distributed alignment search, a supervised interpretability method that learns linear probes/alignments, testing its robustness and susceptibility to overfitting on narrow datasets. They then manipulate the identified components to see if such interventions improve performance on standard acceptability judgment benchmarks.
AI.result: Activation patching reveals a compact, localized, and partially shared set of components in early-to-middle layers that consistently support filler-gap dependency processing across contexts and datasets. In contrast, NPI licensing does not map to a single unified or localized mechanism; its implementation appears more diffuse or heterogeneous across the network. The causally identified filler-gap mechanism generalizes to out-of-distribution data, while the distributed alignment search method tends to overfit, yielding components that do not generalize as well. Intervening on the causally identified components leads to measurable improvements on syntactic acceptability judgment tasks.
AI.conclusion: Language models can implement some syntactic phenomena, like filler-gap dependencies, via relatively compact and shared internal mechanisms, indicating a non-trivial alignment with cross-constructional notions from linguistics. However, not all constructions, such as NPI licensing, share this property, suggesting heterogeneity in how syntax is neurally realized. Causal interpretability methods like activation patching better identify robust, general mechanisms than supervised alignment methods prone to overfitting. Targeted manipulation of these mechanisms can enhance models’ syntactic competence, underscoring the practical and theoretical value of mechanistic analyses for understanding and improving language models.

title: Large Language Models Are Bad Dice Players: LLMs Struggle to Generate Random Numbers from Statistical Distributions
summary: As large language models (LLMs) transition from chat interfaces to integral components of stochastic pipelines and systems approaching general intelligence, the ability to faithfully sample from specified probability distributions has become a functional requirement rather than a theoretical curiosity. We present the first large-scale, statistically powered audit of native probabilistic sampling in frontier LLMs, benchmarking 11 models across 15 distributions. To disentangle failure modes, we employ a dual-protocol design: Batch Generation, where a model produces $N{=}1000$ samples within one response, and Independent Requests, comprising $N{=}1000$ stateless calls. We observe a sharp protocol asymmetry: batch generation achieves only modest statistical validity, with a 7% median pass rate, while independent requests collapse almost entirely, with 10 of 11 models passing none of the distributions. Beyond this asymmetry, we reveal that sampling fidelity degrades monotonically with distributional complexity and aggravates as the sampling horizon $N$ increases. Finally, we demonstrate how the propagation of these failures into downstream real-world application tasks introduces systematic biases: models fail to enforce uniform answer-position constraints in Multiple Choice Question generation and systematically violate demographic targets in attribute-constrained text-to-image prompt synthesis. These findings indicate that current LLMs lack a functional internal sampler, necessitating external tools for applications requiring statistical guarantees.

AI.tldr: The paper audits how well frontier large language models can sample from specified probability distributions and finds they generally fail, especially under independent-request sampling, making them unreliable as internal samplers for applications needing statistical guarantees.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly used as components in complex systems and pipelines, many applications assume they can accurately sample from given probability distributions (e.g., for randomized decisions, controlled generation, or simulation). However, this assumption has not been rigorously tested at scale, and failures could introduce hidden, systematic biases in downstream tasks. The paper aims to fill this gap by empirically evaluating whether LLMs can function as faithful probabilistic samplers.
AI.method: The authors conduct a large-scale audit of 11 frontier LLMs across 15 target probability distributions of varying complexity. They design two protocols: (1) Batch Generation, where each model is prompted once to produce N=1000 samples in a single response; and (2) Independent Requests, where the API is called N=1000 times in a stateless manner, each time requesting a single sample. They then perform statistical tests to assess how closely the empirical samples from each model match the target distributions, analyzing performance differences by protocol, distributional complexity, and sampling horizon N. They further examine how sampling failures manifest in realistic application scenarios such as multiple-choice question generation and attribute-constrained text-to-image prompt creation.
AI.result: The audit shows a strong asymmetry between protocols: under Batch Generation, models achieve only modest statistical validity, with a median pass rate of about 7% across distributions. Under Independent Requests, performance collapses, with 10 out of 11 models failing all distributional tests. Sampling fidelity worsens systematically as the target distributions become more complex and as the number of required samples N grows. In downstream applications, these sampling failures propagate into systematic biases, such as non-uniform answer-position distributions in multiple-choice question generation and violations of demographic mixing targets in constrained text-to-image prompt synthesis.
AI.conclusion: Current frontier LLMs are not reliable probabilistic samplers: they lack a robust internal mechanism for faithfully sampling from user-specified distributions, particularly under stateless, independent-call usage typical of many real systems. As a result, applications that require statistical guarantees or unbiased randomized behavior should not rely solely on LLM-native sampling; instead, they should integrate external, well-tested sampling tools or mechanisms to enforce the desired distributions and avoid hidden biases in downstream tasks.

title: How Large Language Models Balance Internal Knowledge with User and Document Assertions
summary: Large language models (LLMs) often need to balance their internal parametric knowledge with external information, such as user beliefs and content from retrieved documents, in real-world scenarios like RAG or chat-based systems. A model's ability to reliably process these sources is key to system safety. Previous studies on knowledge conflict and sycophancy are limited to a binary conflict paradigm, primarily exploring conflicts between parametric knowledge and either a document or a user, but ignoring the interactive environment where all three sources exist simultaneously. To fill this gap, we propose a three-source interaction framework and systematically evaluate 27 LLMs from 3 families on 2 datasets. Our findings reveal general patterns: most models rely more on document assertions than user assertions, and this preference is reinforced by post-training. Furthermore, our behavioral analysis shows that most models are impressionable, unable to effectively discriminate between helpful and harmful external information. To address this, we demonstrate that fine-tuning on diverse source interaction data can significantly increase a model's discrimination abilities. In short, our work paves the way for developing trustworthy LLMs that can effectively and reliably integrate multiple sources of information. Code is available at https://github.com/shuowl/llm-source-balancing.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how LLMs balance three simultaneous information sources—model knowledge, user beliefs, and retrieved documents—and proposes a framework to evaluate and improve this balancing for safer, more trustworthy systems.
AI.motivation: Existing work on knowledge conflict and sycophancy typically considers only binary conflicts: either model vs. document or model vs. user. However, real-world deployments like RAG and chat assistants involve simultaneous interactions among three sources: the model’s internal knowledge, user-provided assertions, and external documents. There is little systematic understanding of how models behave when these three sources disagree, how post-training affects this behavior, and how to make models better at distinguishing helpful from harmful external information. This gap limits our ability to design safe, reliable LLM systems.
AI.method: The authors propose a three-source interaction framework that explicitly models and tests the interplay among parametric knowledge, user assertions, and document assertions. They construct evaluation setups where these sources can agree or conflict in different ways and use them to systematically benchmark 27 LLMs from three model families on two datasets. They analyze behavioral patterns such as which source the model tends to trust more and how post-training (e.g., instruction tuning or RLHF) shifts these preferences. Finally, they fine-tune models on diverse source-interaction training data to test whether this improves their ability to distinguish beneficial from harmful external inputs.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows consistent patterns across many models: (1) most LLMs prioritize document assertions over user assertions when there is a conflict; and (2) post-training tends to further strengthen this preference for documents. Behavioral analyses also reveal that most models are highly impressionable: they incorporate external information—whether from the user or documents—without adequately judging its reliability, thus failing to robustly differentiate between helpful and harmful content. Fine-tuning on carefully constructed three-source interaction data substantially improves models’ discrimination ability, making them better at choosing which source to trust in conflicting scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that LLMs’ behavior in realistic multi-source environments cannot be fully understood using simple binary conflict settings. Their proposed three-source interaction framework reveals systematic biases—especially an over-reliance on documents and general impressionability—that have important safety implications. However, these behaviors are not fixed: targeted fine-tuning on diverse source-interaction scenarios can markedly enhance models’ judgment about which information to trust. The work thus provides both an analytical lens and a practical training strategy for building more trustworthy LLMs that reliably integrate multiple information sources in applications like RAG and conversational systems.

title: Verbal Confidence Saturation in 3-9B Open-Weight Instruction-Tuned LLMs: A Pre-Registered Psychometric Validity Screen
comment: 10 pages, 3 figures, 4 tables, 1 appendix. Pre-registered: osf.io/azbvx. Code and data: github.com/synthiumjp/koriat
summary: Verbal confidence elicitation is widely used to extract uncertainty estimates from LLMs. We tested whether seven instruction-tuned open-weight models (3-9B parameters, four families) produce verbalised confidence that meets minimal validity criteria for item-level Type-2 discrimination under minimal numeric elicitation with greedy decoding. In a pre-registered study (OSF: osf.io/azbvx), 524 TriviaQA items were administered under numeric (0-100) and categorical (10-class) elicitation to eight models at Q5_K_M quantisation on consumer hardware, yielding 8,384 deterministic trials. A psychometric validity screen was applied to each model-format cell. All seven instruct models were classified Invalid on numeric confidence (H2 confirmed, 7/7 vs. predicted >=4/7), with a mean ceiling rate of 91.7% (H1 confirmed). Categorical elicitation did not rescue validity. Instead, it disrupted task performance in six of seven models, producing accuracy below 5% (H4 not confirmed). Token-level logprobability did not usefully predict verbalised confidence under the observed variance regime (H5 confirmed, mean cross-validated R^2 < 0.01). Within the reasoning-distilled model, reasoning-trace length showed a strong negative partial correlation with confidence (rho = -0.36, p < .001), consistent with the Reasoning Contamination Effect. These results do not imply that internal uncertainty representations are absent. They show that minimal verbal elicitation fails to preserve internal signals at the output interface in this model-size regime. Psychometric screening should precede any downstream use of such signals.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates whether instruction-tuned, open-weight LLMs produce verbally expressed confidence estimates that are psychometrically valid at the item level, and finds that they generally do not under minimal elicitation protocols.
AI.motivation: Verbal confidence ratings from LLMs are increasingly used as proxies for model uncertainty in downstream applications (e.g., self-evaluation, selective prediction, routing). However, it is unclear whether these expressed confidences are actually valid, discriminative measures of uncertainty at the level of individual items, especially for smaller open-weight models and under simple prompting/decoding setups. The authors aim to test whether these widely used setups meet basic psychometric criteria before such signals are trusted or operationalized.
AI.method: The authors conducted a pre-registered psychometric study using 524 questions from TriviaQA. They queried seven instruction-tuned open-weight models (3–9B parameters across four model families), plus one additional model, all at Q5_K_M quantization and with greedy decoding on consumer hardware. Each question was administered under two confidence-elicitation formats: numeric (0–100) and categorical (10 verbal confidence classes). This produced 8,384 deterministic model trials. For each model × format combination, they applied a predefined psychometric validity screen assessing item-level Type-2 discrimination, ceiling effects, and other criteria. They also examined the relationship between token-level log probabilities and expressed confidence, and within one reasoning-distilled model, they analyzed correlations between reasoning-trace length and confidence.
AI.result: All seven instruction-tuned models failed the psychometric validity screen for numeric confidence: they were classified as Invalid, confirming hypothesis H2 (7/7 invalid vs. a preregistered prediction of at least 4/7 invalid). The models showed a very high mean ceiling rate of 91.7%, confirming H1, indicating that they almost always reported maximal or near-maximal confidence. Categorical (10-class) elicitation did not improve psychometric validity; instead, it substantially degraded task performance in six of seven models, decreasing answer accuracy to below 5% and thus failing to confirm H4. Token-level log probabilities did not meaningfully predict the verbalised confidence ratings: the mean cross-validated R² was below 0.01, confirming H5. In the reasoning-distilled model, the authors found a strong negative partial correlation between reasoning-trace length and expressed confidence (rho = -0.36, p < .001), which they interpret as evidence for a Reasoning Contamination Effect.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that, for 3–9B instruction-tuned open-weight models under minimal, commonly used elicitation protocols with greedy decoding, verbalised confidence signals are not psychometrically valid item-level uncertainty estimates. The near-ceiling confidence ratings and lack of Type-2 discrimination suggest that internal uncertainty representations, even if present, are not reliably transmitted through simple verbal confidence outputs. Categorical elicitation not only fails to fix this but can severely harm task performance. Token log probabilities also do not provide a straightforward surrogate for verbal confidence under the tested variance regime. Therefore, before using LLM verbal confidence in any downstream application—such as calibration, abstention, or meta-reasoning—researchers and practitioners should conduct psychometric screening to ensure that these signals carry usable information about uncertainty.

title: Tell Me Why: Designing an Explainable LLM-based Dialogue System for Student Problem Behavior Diagnosis
comment: This paper has been accepted in AIED2026
summary: Diagnosing student problem behaviors requires teachers to synthesize multifaceted information, identify behavioral categories, and plan intervention strategies. Although fine-tuned large language models (LLMs) can support this process through multi-turn dialogue, they rarely explain why a strategy is recommended, limiting transparency and teachers' trust. To address this issue, we present an explainable dialogue system built on a fine-tuned LLM. The system uses a hierarchical attribution method based on explainable AI (xAI) to identify dialogue evidence for each recommendation and generate a natural-language explanation based on that evidence. In technical evaluation, the method outperformed baseline approaches in identifying supporting evidence. In a preliminary user study with 22 pre-service teachers, participants who received explanations reported higher trust in the system. These findings suggest a promising direction for improving LLM explainability in educational dialogue systems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an explainable LLM-based dialogue system that helps teachers diagnose and address student problem behaviors, providing explicit evidence-based explanations for its recommendations.
AI.motivation: Teachers must integrate complex behavioral information and choose interventions, but current LLM-based assistants lack transparent justifications for their advice, which undermines teacher trust and limits adoption.
AI.method: The authors fine-tune a large language model for multi-turn dialogue about student behavior and augment it with a hierarchical attribution xAI mechanism that identifies which dialogue segments support each recommendation, then converts this evidence into natural-language explanations. They technically evaluate evidence identification against baselines and run a user study with pre-service teachers.
AI.result: The hierarchical attribution approach more accurately pinpoints supporting dialogue evidence than baseline methods. In a user study with 22 pre-service teachers, those who saw generated explanations reported higher trust in the system than those who did not.
AI.conclusion: Combining fine-tuned LLMs with hierarchical attribution-based explanations improves transparency and user trust in educational dialogue systems, indicating a viable path toward more explainable AI support for classroom behavior diagnosis and intervention planning.

title: Navigating Large-Scale Document Collections: MuDABench for Multi-Document Analytical QA
comment: Findings of ACL 2026. The camera-ready version corrects some labeling errors. The accompanying repository is continuously updated based on community feedback; for the most up-to-date implementation and results, please refer to the repository
summary: This paper introduces the task of analytical question answering over large, semi-structured document collections. We present MuDABench, a benchmark for multi-document analytical QA, where questions require extracting and synthesizing information across numerous documents to perform quantitative analysis. Unlike existing multi-document QA benchmarks that typically require information from only a few documents with limited cross-document reasoning, MuDABench demands extensive inter-document analysis and aggregation. Constructed via distant supervision by leveraging document-level metadata and annotated financial databases, MuDABench comprises over 80,000 pages and 332 analytical QA instances. We also propose an evaluation protocol that measures final answer accuracy and uses intermediate-fact coverage as an auxiliary diagnostic signal for the reasoning process. Experiments reveal that standard RAG systems, which treat all documents as a flat retrieval pool, perform poorly. To address these limitations, we propose a multi-agent workflow that orchestrates planning, extraction, and code generation modules. While this approach substantially improves both process and outcome metrics, a significant gap remains compared to human expert performance. Our analysis identifies two primary bottlenecks: single-document information extraction accuracy and insufficient domain-specific knowledge in current systems. MuDABench is available at https://github.com/Zhanli-Li/MuDABench.

AI.tldr: MuDABench is a new benchmark for multi-document analytical QA over large semi-structured financial document collections, plus a multi-agent method and evaluation protocol; current systems lag far behind humans.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-document QA benchmarks only require reasoning over a handful of documents and limited cross-document aggregation, which does not reflect real-world analytical tasks (e.g., financial analysis) that require synthesizing quantitative information from many documents. There is a lack of benchmarks and methods specifically targeting this kind of large-scale, analytical multi-document QA.
AI.method: 1) Construct MuDABench via distant supervision using document-level metadata and annotated financial databases, producing >80K pages and 332 analytical questions that require quantitative, cross-document aggregation. 2) Design an evaluation protocol combining final answer accuracy with intermediate-fact coverage to diagnose reasoning quality. 3) Benchmark standard RAG systems that treat all documents as a flat retrieval pool. 4) Propose a multi-agent workflow that coordinates planning, information extraction, and code-generation components for better analytical reasoning over many documents.
AI.result: Standard RAG approaches perform poorly on MuDABench, showing they struggle with large-scale analytical QA. The proposed multi-agent workflow significantly improves both final-answer accuracy and intermediate-fact coverage relative to standard RAG baselines, but still underperforms human experts by a substantial margin. Error analysis highlights issues with per-document extraction quality and lack of domain-specific financial knowledge.
AI.conclusion: MuDABench exposes substantial shortcomings of current multi-document QA systems on realistic analytical tasks that require large-scale, quantitative reasoning. The multi-agent workflow is a promising direction but not yet sufficient to reach human-level performance. Future improvements must address more accurate single-document extraction and better integration of domain-specific knowledge. MuDABench and its evaluation framework provide a valuable testbed for such advances.

title: Bridging the Long-Tail Gap: Robust Retrieval-Augmented Relation Completion via Multi-Stage Paraphrase Infusion
summary: Large language models (LLMs) struggle with relation completion (RC), both with and without retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), particularly when the required information is rare or sparsely represented. To address this, we propose a novel multi-stage paraphrase-guided relation-completion framework, RC-RAG, that systematically incorporates relation paraphrases across multiple stages. In particular, RC-RAG: (a) integrates paraphrases into retrieval to expand lexical coverage of the relation, (b) uses paraphrases to generate relation-aware summaries, and (c) leverages paraphrases during generation to guide reasoning for relation completion. Importantly, our method does not require any model fine-tuning. Experiments with five LLMs on two benchmark datasets show that RC-RAG consistently outperforms several RAG baselines. In long-tail settings, the best-performing LLM augmented with RC-RAG improves by 40.6 Exact Match (EM) points over its standalone performance and surpasses two strong RAG baselines by 16.0 and 13.8 EM points, respectively, while maintaining low computational overhead.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RC-RAG, a paraphrase-guided, multi-stage retrieval-augmented framework that significantly improves long-tail relation completion performance of LLMs without fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: LLMs perform poorly on relation completion, especially when the target facts are rare or sparsely expressed in text. Standard RAG pipelines also struggle because they depend heavily on lexical overlap between the query and documents and lack explicit guidance about the target relation, leading to low recall and weak reasoning in long-tail scenarios. There is a need for a method that can better capture diverse surface forms of relations and use them throughout the RAG pipeline without necessitating costly fine-tuning.
AI.method: The authors propose RC-RAG, a multi-stage paraphrase-guided framework for relation completion. It systematically generates or uses multiple paraphrases of a given relation and injects them into three stages: (a) Retrieval: relation paraphrases are added as alternative queries or query expansions to improve lexical coverage and retrieve evidence expressed in varied wording; (b) Summarization: the retrieved passages are distilled into relation-aware summaries conditioned on the paraphrases, helping the model focus on content relevant to the target relation; (c) Generation: during answer generation, the paraphrases are provided as guidance so the LLM can reason explicitly about the intended relation and synthesize a final answer. The framework is model-agnostic and operates without any fine-tuning of the underlying LLMs.
AI.result: On two benchmark relation-completion datasets and across five different LLMs, RC-RAG consistently outperforms several strong RAG baselines. In long-tail settings, the best-performing LLM combined with RC-RAG gains 40.6 Exact Match points compared to using the LLM alone and exceeds two competitive RAG baselines by 16.0 and 13.8 EM points, respectively. These improvements are achieved while keeping computational overhead low.
AI.conclusion: RC-RAG demonstrates that systematically incorporating relation paraphrases into retrieval, intermediate summarization, and final generation substantially boosts LLM performance on relation completion tasks, particularly for rare or sparsely documented facts, without requiring model fine-tuning. The framework offers an efficient, model-agnostic way to enhance RAG systems for long-tail relational knowledge extraction.

title: Large Language Models Decide Early and Explain Later
summary: Large Language Models often achieve strong performance by generating long intermediate chain-of-thought reasoning. However, it remains unclear when a model's final answer is actually determined during generation. If the answer is already fixed at an intermediate stage, subsequent reasoning tokens may constitute post-decision explanation, increasing inference cost and latency without improving correctness. We study the evolution of predicted answers over reasoning steps using forced answer completion, which elicits the model's intermediate predictions at partial reasoning prefixes. Focusing on Qwen3-4B and averaging results across all datasets considered, we find that predicted answers change in only 32% of queries. Moreover, once the final answer switch occurs, the model generates an average of 760 additional reasoning tokens per query, accounting for a substantial fraction of the total reasoning budget. Motivated by these findings, we investigate early stopping strategies that halt generation once the answer has stabilized. We show that simple heuristics, including probe-based stopping, can reduce reasoning token usage by 500 tokens per query while incurring only a 2% drop in accuracy. Together, our results indicate that a large portion of chain-of-thought generation is redundant and can be reduced with minimal impact on performance.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes when during chain-of-thought generation a model’s answer becomes fixed, finds that most later reasoning is redundant, and proposes early-stopping strategies that cut tokens with minimal accuracy loss.
AI.motivation: Chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning in large language models improves performance but produces long explanations, which are costly and slow at inference time. It is unknown at which point in the reasoning process the model’s final answer is actually determined. If the answer stabilizes early, much of the remaining CoT may just be post-hoc justification and therefore an inefficient use of computation. Understanding this dynamic can enable methods to trim unnecessary reasoning tokens while preserving accuracy.
AI.method: The authors examine how a model’s predicted answer evolves across the steps of chain-of-thought generation using a technique they call forced answer completion. At multiple partial prefixes of the reasoning, they force the model to commit to an answer and record it, tracking how often and when answers change. They run this analysis on Qwen3-4B across multiple datasets, measuring the frequency of answer switches and the number of tokens produced after the final switch. Based on these observations, they then design and evaluate early stopping strategies—such as simple heuristics and probe-based criteria—that terminate reasoning once the predicted answer appears stable, and assess the tradeoff between reduced token usage and accuracy.
AI.result: Across all datasets, the model’s predicted answers change in only about 32% of queries during the reasoning process, indicating that most answers are fixed early. After the last change of answer, the model still generates on average around 760 more reasoning tokens per query, a large share of the total CoT budget. Their early stopping strategies, including probe-based stopping rules, are able to cut roughly 500 reasoning tokens per query while causing only about a 2% drop in accuracy, demonstrating that substantial portions of CoT can be removed with limited performance degradation.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that much of chain-of-thought generation is redundant post-decision explanation. The model’s final answer is often determined well before reasoning ends, and substantial token savings are possible by halting generation once the answer stabilizes. Simple early-stopping heuristics, including probe-based methods, can significantly reduce reasoning token usage and latency with only minor accuracy losses. This suggests a practical path for making CoT-based inference more efficient without heavily sacrificing performance.

title: STEM: Structure-Tracing Evidence Mining for Knowledge Graphs-Driven Retrieval-Augmented Generation
comment: 34 pages, 16 figures, accepted to ACL 2026 (Main Conference)
summary: Knowledge Graph-based Question Answering (KGQA) plays a pivotal role in complex reasoning tasks but remains constrained by two persistent challenges: the structural heterogeneity of Knowledge Graphs(KGs) often leads to semantic mismatch during retrieval, while existing reasoning path retrieval methods lack a global structural perspective. To address these issues, we propose Structure-Tracing Evidence Mining (STEM), a novel framework that reframes multi-hop reasoning as a schema-guided graph search task. First, we design a Semantic-to-Structural Projection pipeline that leverages KG structural priors to decompose queries into atomic relational assertions and construct an adaptive query schema graph. Subsequently, we execute globally-aware node anchoring and subgraph retrieval to obtain the final evidence reasoning graph from KG. To more effectively integrate global structural information during the graph construction process, we design a Triple-Dependent GNN (Triple-GNN) to generate a Global Guidance Subgraph (Guidance Graph) that guides the construction. STEM significantly improves both the accuracy and evidence completeness of multi-hop reasoning graph retrieval, and achieves State-of-the-Art performance on multiple multi-hop benchmarks.

AI.tldr: They propose STEM, a schema-guided graph search framework that uses KG structure and a Triple-Dependent GNN to build better multi-hop reasoning graphs, improving KGQA accuracy and evidence completeness.
AI.motivation: KGQA for multi-hop reasoning suffers from (1) semantic mismatch due to structural heterogeneity of KGs when retrieving facts and (2) path retrieval methods that only consider local paths without a global KG structure view. These issues reduce both answer accuracy and the completeness of the reasoning evidence.
AI.method: They introduce STEM, which reframes multi-hop KGQA as a schema-guided graph search. A Semantic-to-Structural Projection pipeline decomposes a natural language query into atomic relational assertions and builds an adaptive query schema graph using KG structural priors. Then, they perform globally-aware node anchoring and subgraph retrieval to obtain an evidence reasoning graph from the KG. To inject global structural information, they propose a Triple-Dependent GNN (Triple-GNN) that constructs a Global Guidance Subgraph (Guidance Graph) that steers the search and graph construction.
AI.result: STEM yields more accurate and more complete multi-hop reasoning graphs compared to prior methods and achieves new state-of-the-art performance on several multi-hop KGQA benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Schema-guided, structure-tracing search combined with Triple-GNN–based global structural guidance effectively alleviates semantic mismatch and local-path bias in multi-hop KGQA, leading to superior reasoning graph retrieval and overall KGQA performance on standard benchmarks.

title: ReLeVAnT: Relevance Lexical Vectors for Accurate Legal Text Classification
summary: The classification of legal documents from an unstructured data corpus has several crucial applications in downstream tasks. Documents relevant to court filings are key in use cases such as drafting motions, memos, and outlines, as well as in tasks like docket summarisation, retrieval systems, and training data curation. Current methods classify based on provided metadata, LLM-extracted metadata, or multimodal methods. These methods depend on structured data, metadata, and extensive computational power. This task is approached from a perspective of leveraging discriminative features in the documents between classes. The authors propose ReLeVAnT, a framework for legal document binary classification. ReLeVAnT utilises n-gram processing, contrastive score matching, and a shallow neural network as the primary drivers for discriminative classification. It leverages one-time keyword extraction per corpus, followed by a shallow classifier to swiftly and reliably classify documents with 99.3% accuracy and 98.7% F1 score on the LexGLUE dataset.

AI.tldr: A lightweight framework (ReLeVAnT) for binary classification of legal documents that avoids reliance on metadata and heavy models, using n-grams, contrastive score matching, and a shallow neural network to achieve state-of-the-art performance on LexGLUE.
AI.motivation: Legal-tech applications like drafting motions, docket summarisation, information retrieval, and dataset curation require fast, accurate classification of relevant court-related documents from large, unstructured corpora. Existing approaches often assume access to structured metadata, LLM-based annotations, or multimodal inputs, and can be computationally expensive or infeasible when such metadata is missing or noisy. There is a need for a method that works directly on raw text, is discriminative, efficient, and still highly accurate.
AI.method: The authors introduce ReLeVAnT, a binary text classification framework that identifies discriminative features between document classes. It first performs one-time keyword extraction across the corpus using n-gram processing and contrastive score matching to find n-grams that best distinguish positive from negative class documents. These extracted features are then fed into a shallow neural network classifier, enabling efficient training and inference without large language models or complex multimodal architectures.
AI.result: On the LexGLUE benchmark dataset, ReLeVAnT achieves 99.3% accuracy and 98.7% F1 score for the binary classification task, indicating very strong performance. The framework is also computationally lightweight, as it relies on precomputed n-gram features and a shallow neural network rather than large transformer models or extensive metadata processing.
AI.conclusion: ReLeVAnT shows that a carefully designed, feature-focused pipeline using n-grams, contrastive scoring, and a shallow neural network can match or exceed the performance of heavier, metadata-dependent systems for legal document binary classification. This suggests that high-quality, scalable legal document classification can be achieved without relying on LLMs or multimodal architectures, provided that discriminative textual features are effectively extracted and leveraged.

title: Contexts are Never Long Enough: Structured Reasoning for Scalable Question Answering over Long Document Sets
summary: Real-world document question answering is challenging. Analysts must synthesize evidence across multiple documents and different parts of each document. However, any fixed LLM context window can be exceeded as document collections grow. A common workaround is to decompose documents into chunks and assemble answers from chunk-level outputs, but this introduces an aggregation bottleneck: as the number of chunks grows, systems must still combine and reason over an increasingly large body of extracted evidence. We present SLIDERS, a framework for question answering over long document collections through structured reasoning. SLIDERS extracts salient information into a relational database, enabling scalable reasoning over persistent structured state via SQL rather than concatenated text. To make this locally extracted representation globally coherent, SLIDERS introduces a data reconciliation stage that leverages provenance, extraction rationales, and metadata to detect and repair duplicated, inconsistent, and incomplete records. SLIDERS outperforms all baselines on three existing long-context benchmarks, despite all of them fitting within the context window of strong base LLMs, exceeding GPT-4.1 by 6.6 points on average. It also improves over the next best baseline by ~19 and ~32 points on two new benchmarks at 3.9M and 36M tokens, respectively.

AI.tldr: SLIDERS is a framework for long-document question answering that turns extracted evidence into a relational database and reconciles it, enabling scalable, structured reasoning that outperforms strong LLM baselines on very large contexts.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based document QA struggles when document collections exceed the context window. Chunking documents alleviates context limits but creates an aggregation bottleneck: as chunks grow, models must still reason over a large, unstructured set of extracted snippets, leading to scalability and coherence issues. The authors want a method that scales to millions of tokens while maintaining global consistency and strong QA performance.
AI.method: SLIDERS first uses an LLM (or extractor) to pull salient facts from documents into a structured, relational database schema instead of keeping them as free-form text. It then performs a data reconciliation stage that uses provenance, extraction rationales, and metadata to detect and fix duplicate, inconsistent, or incomplete records, yielding a coherent global representation. Question answering is then executed as structured reasoning over this database via SQL queries and subsequent LLM steps, rather than by concatenating all relevant text into the context window.
AI.result: On three existing long-context QA benchmarks that already fit within strong LLMs’ context windows, SLIDERS still outperforms all baselines, beating GPT-4.1 by an average of 6.6 points. On two newly introduced, much larger benchmarks of about 3.9M and 36M tokens, SLIDERS substantially outperforms the next best baseline by approximately 19 and 32 points, respectively.
AI.conclusion: Structured, database-backed representations with reconciliation can overcome the aggregation bottleneck in long-document QA. By extracting, cleaning, and reasoning over persistent structured state rather than raw text chunks, SLIDERS scales to very large document collections and yields significantly better performance than strong context-based LLM baselines across both existing and new benchmarks.

title: CNSL-bench: Benchmarking the Sign Language Understanding Capabilities of MLLMs on Chinese National Sign Language
comment: Accepted as the Main Conference at ACL 2026
summary: Sign language research has achieved significant progress due to the advances in large language models (LLMs). However, the intrinsic ability of LLMs to understand sign language, especially in multimodal contexts, remains underexplored. To address this limitation, we introduce CNSL-bench, the first comprehensive Chinese em{National Sign Language benchmark designed for evaluating multimodal large language models (MLLMs) in sign language understanding. The proposed CNSL-bench is characterized by: 1) Authoritative grounding, as it is anchored to the officially standardized \textit{National Common Sign Language Dictionary, mitigating ambiguity from regional or non-canonical variants and ensuring consistent semantic definitions; 2) Multimodal coverage, providing aligned textual descriptions, illustrative images, and sign language videos; and 3) Articulatory diversity, supporting fine-grained analysis across key manual articulatory forms, including air-writing, finger-spelling, and the Chinese manual-alphabet. Using CNSL-bench, we extensively evaluate 21 open-source and proprietary up-to-date MLLMs. Our results reveal that, despite recent advances in multimodal modeling, current MLLMs remain substantially inferior to human performance, exhibiting systematic disparities across input modalities and manual articulatory forms. Additional diagnostic analyses suggest that several performance limitations persist beyond improvements in reasoning and that instruction-following robustness varies substantially across models.

AI.tldr: Introduces CNSL-bench, the first comprehensive Chinese National Sign Language benchmark to evaluate multimodal large language models on sign language understanding, and shows current models lag far behind humans.
AI.motivation: Although large language models have advanced sign language research, we lack a clear understanding and a standardized way to evaluate how well multimodal LLMs intrinsically understand sign language, especially Chinese National Sign Language, across different modalities and articulatory forms.
AI.method: Construct a benchmark (CNSL-bench) grounded in the official National Common Sign Language Dictionary, compiling aligned text, images, and sign videos, and organizing data by different manual articulatory forms (air-writing, finger-spelling, Chinese manual alphabet). Then evaluate 21 state-of-the-art open-source and proprietary multimodal LLMs on this benchmark and perform diagnostic analyses of their performance patterns and instruction-following robustness.
AI.result: CNSL-bench enables systematic testing of multimodal LLMs on Chinese National Sign Language. Experiments on 21 contemporary models show that they perform far worse than human evaluators and exhibit consistent weaknesses depending on the input modality (text/image/video) and the articulatory form of the signs.
AI.conclusion: Current multimodal LLMs, despite overall progress, still lack robust and comprehensive understanding of Chinese National Sign Language. Performance gaps relative to humans and modality- and articulatory-specific weaknesses persist, and many limitations are not solved simply by better reasoning capabilities; instruction-following reliability also varies a lot between models, underscoring the need for targeted improvement in sign language modeling.

title: CLARITY: A Framework and Benchmark for Conversational Language Ambiguity and Unanswerability in Interactive NL2SQL Systems
summary:
NL2SQL systems deployed in industry settings often encounter ambiguous or unanswerable queries, particularly in interactive scenarios with incomplete user clarification. Existing benchmarks typically assume a single source of ambiguity and rely on user interaction for resolution, overlooking realistic failure modes.
  We introduce Clarity, a framework for automatically generating an NL2SQL benchmark with multi-faceted ambiguities and diverse user behaviors across both single- and multi-turn settings. Using a constraint-driven pipeline, Clarity transforms executable SQL into ambiguous queries, augmented with grounded conversational continuations and schema-level metadata.
  Empirical evaluation on Spider and BIRD shows that leading NL2SQL systems, including those based on strong LLMs, suffer significant performance degradation under multi-faceted ambiguity. While these systems often detect ambiguity, they struggle to accurately localize and resolve the underlying schema-level sources. Our results highlight the need for more robust ambiguity detection and resolution in industry-grade NL2SQL systems.

AI.tldr: The paper presents Clarity, a framework to generate realistic NL2SQL benchmarks that expose multi-faceted ambiguities and diverse user behaviors, revealing that current NL2SQL systems, even LLM-based ones, degrade significantly under such conditions.
AI.motivation: Industrial NL2SQL systems frequently face ambiguous or unanswerable queries, especially in interactive settings with incomplete user clarification. Existing benchmarks idealize ambiguity by assuming a single source and relying on perfect user interaction, thus failing to capture real-world failure modes. There is a need for evaluation resources that better reflect complex, multi-faceted ambiguity and user behavior patterns.
AI.method: The authors design Clarity, a framework that automatically converts executable SQL queries into ambiguous natural language queries via a constraint-driven pipeline. It injects multiple, interacting ambiguity types, and augments each query with grounded conversational continuations that simulate diverse user behaviors, along with schema-level metadata. Clarity supports both single-turn and multi-turn interactions and is instantiated on existing datasets such as Spider and BIRD.
AI.result: When applying Clarity-generated benchmarks on Spider and BIRD, state-of-the-art NL2SQL systems, including ones built on strong large language models, show substantial drops in performance compared to standard settings. The systems are often able to flag that a query is ambiguous but fail to pinpoint the exact schema-level source(s) of ambiguity and to resolve them correctly in conversation.
AI.conclusion: Clarity exposes substantial gaps in current NL2SQL systems by demonstrating their vulnerability to realistic, multi-faceted ambiguities and imperfect user interactions. The study suggests that advancing industry-grade NL2SQL requires better mechanisms for ambiguity detection, precise localization of schema-level issues, and effective resolution strategies, and that Clarity can serve as a more realistic benchmark for future research and system development.

title: Controllable Spoken Dialogue Generation: An LLM-Driven Grading System for K-12 Non-Native English Learners
summary:
Large language models (LLMs) often fail to meet the pedagogical needs of K-12 English learners in non-native contexts due to a proficiency mismatch. To address this widespread challenge, we introduce a proficiency-aligned framework that adapts LLM outputs to learner abilities, using China's national curriculum (CSE) as a representative case. Our framework enables precise control over lexical complexity through a four-tier grading system, supported by a comprehensive suite of new resources: graded vocabulary lists and a multi-turn dialogue corpus.
  Our core technical contribution is the \textbf{DDPO} algorithm,Diversity Driven Policy Optimization, a multi-turn GRPO-based approach designed to preserve dialogue diversity while holistically optimizing dialogue quality. This method significantly outperforms conventional approaches, achieving low out-of-vocabulary rates and high diversity while enhancing conversational naturalness and pedagogical value. While grounded in the CSE, our framework is designed for flexibility and can be readily adapted to other educational standards. Our models, data, and code will all be open-sourced, providing a scalable platform for personalized English speaking practice that effectively addresses the unique challenges faced by K-12 learners in non-immersive environments.

AI.tldr: They propose a framework and algorithm (DDPO) to adapt LLM dialogues to K-12 English learners’ proficiency levels, controlling lexical complexity using China’s CSE standard, with new graded resources and better diversity/quality trade-offs.
AI.motivation: LLMs usually respond with language that is too difficult or misaligned for K-12 English learners, especially in non-English-speaking environments, limiting their usefulness for practice and instruction. There is a need for methods that align model output with learners’ proficiency levels while maintaining natural, engaging dialogues.
AI.method: They design a proficiency-aligned framework tied to the CSE curriculum with four lexical-complexity tiers, supported by graded vocabulary lists and a multi-turn dialogue corpus. Technically, they introduce DDPO (Diversity Driven Policy Optimization), a GRPO-based multi-turn training algorithm that explicitly optimizes both dialogue diversity and multiple aspects of dialogue quality while enforcing lexical constraints to match proficiency levels.
AI.result: The DDPO-based models achieve lower out-of-vocabulary rates relative to the graded word lists, higher dialogue diversity, and improved conversational naturalness and pedagogical usefulness compared with conventional optimization approaches. The framework demonstrates effective control of lexical complexity in multi-turn dialogues for K-12 learners.
AI.conclusion: A proficiency-aligned LLM framework, instantiated with China’s CSE but adaptable to other standards, can generate controlled-complexity, diverse, and educationally valuable dialogues for young English learners. The open-sourced models, data, and code provide a scalable basis for personalized speaking practice in non-immersive contexts.

title: Dynamically Acquiring Text Content to Enable the Classification of Lesser-known Entities for Real-world Tasks
summary: Existing Natural Language Processing (NLP) resources often lack the task-specific information required for real-world problems and provide limited coverage of lesser-known or newly introduced entities. For example, business organizations and health care providers may need to be classified into a variety of different taxonomic schemes for specific application tasks. Our goal is to enable domain experts to easily create a task-specific classifier for entities by providing only entity names and gold labels as training data. Our framework then dynamically acquires descriptive text about each entity, which is subsequently used as the basis for producing a text-based classifier. We propose a novel text acquisition method that leverages both web and large language models (LLMs). We evaluate our proposed framework on two classification problems in distinct domains: (i) classifying organizations into Standard Industrial Classification (SIC) Codes, which categorize organizations based on their business activities; and (ii) classifying healthcare providers into healthcare provider taxonomy codes, which represent a provider's medical specialty and area of practice. Our best-performing model achieved macro-averaged F1-scores of 82.3% and 72.9% on the SIC code and healthcare taxonomy code classification tasks, respectively.
