AI.tldr: They build a system that lets domain experts train task-specific entity classifiers using only entity names and labels, by automatically gathering descriptive text via web search and LLMs, and then training a text-based classifier, achieving strong F1 scores on organization and healthcare provider taxonomy tasks.
AI.motivation: General NLP resources and off-the-shelf models lack fine-grained, task-specific, and up-to-date coverage for many real-world classification needs, such as mapping organizations or healthcare providers into detailed taxonomy codes. Manually curating such resources is costly and slow, and many entities are obscure or newly introduced, so their information is missing in standard corpora. The authors want to lower the barrier for domain experts to build custom, high-quality entity classifiers without needing large, curated text corpora or NLP expertise.
AI.method: Allow domain experts to supply only entity names and corresponding gold labels as training data. The framework then automatically and dynamically retrieves descriptive text for each entity using a new text acquisition method that combines web resources and large language models (LLMs). These acquired descriptions are used as input features to train a text-based classifier tailored to the specific classification task. The method is evaluated on two tasks: classifying business organizations into SIC codes and healthcare providers into taxonomy codes.
AI.result: The framework successfully learns task-specific classifiers using only entity names plus labels, thanks to the automatically acquired descriptive text. On two benchmarks, the best-performing model achieves macro-averaged F1 scores of 82.3% for classifying organizations into Standard Industrial Classification (SIC) codes and 72.9% for classifying healthcare providers into healthcare provider taxonomy codes, indicating strong performance in both domains.
AI.conclusion: Automatically acquiring descriptive text via web and LLMs from only entity names and labels is an effective way to build task-specific entity classifiers, even in domains where standard NLP resources are sparse or outdated. The approach reduces the need for manual corpus construction or extensive feature engineering while attaining strong performance on real-world taxonomy classification tasks. This suggests that similar techniques could be applied broadly to other domains requiring custom, fine-grained entity classification.

title: Learning Evidence Highlighting for Frozen LLMs
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) can reason well, yet often miss decisive evidence when it is buried in long, noisy contexts. We introduce HiLight, an Evidence Emphasis framework that decouples evidence selection from reasoning for frozen LLM solvers. HiLight avoids compressing or rewriting the input, which can discard or distort evidence, by training a lightweight Emphasis Actor to insert minimal highlight tags around pivotal spans in the unaltered context. A frozen Solver then performs downstream reasoning on the emphasized input. We cast highlighting as a weakly supervised decision-making problem and optimize the Actor with reinforcement learning using only the Solver's task reward, requiring no evidence labels and no access to or modification of the Solver. Across sequential recommendation and long-context question answering, HiLight consistently improves performance over strong prompt-based and automated prompt-optimization baselines. The learned emphasis policy transfers zero-shot to both smaller and larger unseen Solver families, including an API-based Solver, suggesting that the Actor captures genuine, reusable evidence structure rather than overfitting to a single backbone.

AI.tldr: HiLight trains a small model to highlight key evidence spans in long, noisy contexts so a frozen LLM can reason better without changing its text.
AI.motivation: LLMs struggle to reliably find and use crucial evidence when it is buried in long or noisy inputs. Existing approaches that compress or rewrite context risk discarding or distorting important information and often require modifying or tightly coupling to the main LLM. There is a need for a lightweight, model-agnostic way to help frozen LLMs focus on pivotal evidence in long contexts without supervision on what counts as evidence.
AI.method: The paper proposes HiLight, which separates evidence selection from reasoning. A lightweight Emphasis Actor is trained to insert minimal highlight tags around potentially important spans directly into the original context, leaving the text otherwise unchanged. A frozen Solver LLM then performs the final reasoning on this emphasized input. Highlighting is framed as a weakly supervised decision-making problem: the Actor is optimized via reinforcement learning using only the Solver’s task reward as feedback, with no explicit evidence labels and no modification or gradient access to the Solver.
AI.result: On tasks in sequential recommendation and long-context question answering, HiLight improves the performance of frozen LLM Solvers compared to strong prompt-based and automatic prompt-optimization baselines. The learned highlighting policy generalizes zero-shot across different Solver families, including both smaller and larger models and an external API-based Solver, while maintaining or improving task accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling evidence emphasis from reasoning through a learned highlighting policy can reliably boost the performance of frozen LLMs on long-context tasks without requiring evidence annotations or Solver modification. The Actor appears to capture reusable structure about what constitutes pivotal evidence, enabling transfer across models and suggesting a practical, model-agnostic path to improve long-context reasoning in LLM applications.

title: Context-Fidelity Boosting: Enhancing Faithful Generation through Watermark-Inspired Decoding
summary: Large language models (LLMs) often produce content that contradicts or overlooks information provided in the input context, a phenomenon known as faithfulness hallucination. In this paper, we propose Context-Fidelity Boosting (CFB), a lightweight and general decoding-time framework that reduces such hallucinations by increasing the generation probability of source-supported tokens. Motivated by logit-shaping principles from watermarking techniques, CFB applies additive token-level logit adjustments based on a token's degree of support from the input context. Specifically, we develop three boosting strategies: static boosting, which applies a fixed bias to source-supported tokens; context-aware boosting, which scales this bias using the divergence between next-token distributions with and without context; and token-aware boosting, which further redistributes the adaptive bias according to local relevance estimated from source-position attention and source-scoped semantic similarity. CFB requires no retraining or architectural changes, making it compatible with a wide range of LLMs. Experiments on summarization and question answering tasks across multiple open-source LLMs show that CFB consistently improves faithfulness metrics with minimal generation overhead. Our implementation is fully open-sourced.

AI.tldr: They propose CFB, a decoding-time framework to reduce faithfulness hallucinations in LLMs by boosting probabilities of context-supported tokens, improving faithfulness on summarization and QA without retraining.
AI.motivation: LLMs hallucinate by contradicting or ignoring given context, which is problematic for tasks like summarization and QA that require faithfulness. Existing fixes often need retraining or architectural changes; a lightweight, general, decoding-time solution is desirable.
AI.method: Context-Fidelity Boosting (CFB) adjusts token logits at decoding based on how much each token is supported by the input context. It introduces three strategies: (1) static boosting—adds a fixed logit bias to tokens supported by the source; (2) context-aware boosting—scales this bias using the divergence between next-token distributions with vs. without context; (3) token-aware boosting—further redistributes the adaptive bias based on local relevance from attention over source positions and semantic similarity restricted to the source. No model retraining or architecture changes are required.
AI.result: Across summarization and QA benchmarks on multiple open-source LLMs, CFB consistently improves faithfulness-related metrics while adding only small decoding overhead.
AI.conclusion: Decoding-time logit shaping guided by context support effectively reduces faithfulness hallucinations in LLMs in a model-agnostic way. CFB offers a practical, plug-and-play mechanism to boost contextual faithfulness without modifying or retraining LLMs, and the released implementation facilitates adoption and further research.

title: CRAFT: Clustered Regression for Adaptive Filtering of Training data
summary: Selecting a small, high-quality subset from a large corpus for fine-tuning is increasingly important as corpora grow to tens of millions of datapoints, making full fine-tuning expensive and often unnecessary. We propose CRAFT (Clustered Regression for Adaptive Filtering of Training data), a vectorization-agnostic selection method for training sequence-to-sequence models. CRAFT decomposes the joint source-target distribution and performs a two-stage selection: (i) match the validation source distribution through proportional budget allocation across k-means clusters, and (ii) within each source cluster, select training pairs whose target embeddings minimize a conditional expected distance derived from the validation target distribution. We prove that proportional cluster allocation bounds the continuous KL divergence between selected and validation distributions, with the residual controlled by cluster diameters. We evaluate CRAFT on English-Hindi translation by selecting training data from 33 million NLLB sentence pairs and fine-tuning mBART via LoRA. CRAFT achieves 43.34 BLEU, outperforming TSDS (41.21) by 2.13 points on the same candidate pool and encoder while completing selection over 40 times faster. With TF-IDF vectorization, the entire pipeline completes in under one minute on CPU. TAROT achieves 45.61 BLEU, but CRAFT completes selection in 26.86 seconds versus TAROT's 75.6 seconds, a 2.8 time speedup.

AI.tldr: Proposes CRAFT, a fast, vectorization-agnostic data selection method that chooses high-quality subsets from massive corpora for seq2seq fine-tuning, achieving strong BLEU with large speedups over prior methods.
AI.motivation: Fully fine-tuning on tens of millions of datapoints is computationally expensive and often unnecessary because only a subset is needed to capture the relevant distribution for a target task. Existing data selection methods can be slow, tied to specific vectorizations, or suboptimal in matching the validation distribution. There is a need for an efficient, theoretically grounded method to select a small but high-quality subset of training data that closely matches the validation distribution for sequence-to-sequence tasks like machine translation.
AI.method: The paper introduces CRAFT (Clustered Regression for Adaptive Filtering of Training data), a vectorization-agnostic selection algorithm for seq2seq models. It decomposes the joint source-target distribution and uses a two-stage process: (1) apply k-means clustering on source-side representations and allocate the selection budget proportionally across clusters to match the validation source distribution; (2) within each cluster, select training pairs whose target-side embeddings minimize a conditional expected distance derived from the validation target distribution. The authors prove that proportional allocation across clusters bounds the KL divergence between selected and validation distributions, with residual divergence governed by cluster diameters. The method is compatible with different vectorizations (e.g., TF-IDF, encoder embeddings).
AI.result: On English–Hindi machine translation, selecting from 33M NLLB sentence pairs and fine-tuning mBART with LoRA, CRAFT attains 43.34 BLEU, surpassing TSDS (41.21) by 2.13 BLEU under the same candidate pool and encoder. CRAFT performs selection more than 40× faster than TSDS. Using simple TF-IDF vectorization, the entire pipeline runs in under one minute on CPU. Compared with TAROT, which achieves 45.61 BLEU, CRAFT is slightly worse in BLEU but completes selection in 26.86 seconds versus 75.6 seconds, a 2.8× speedup.
AI.conclusion: CRAFT provides an efficient, theoretically supported, and vectorization-agnostic method for selecting high-quality subsets from very large corpora for seq2seq fine-tuning. It substantially accelerates data selection while maintaining or improving translation quality relative to strong baselines, making it practical to use massive candidate pools with modest computational resources.

title: Preference Heads in Large Language Models: A Mechanistic Framework for Interpretable Personalization
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) exhibit strong implicit personalization ability, yet most existing approaches treat this behavior as a black box, relying on prompt engineering or fine tuning on user data. In this work, we adopt a mechanistic interpretability perspective and hypothesize the existence of a sparse set of Preference Heads, attention heads that encode user specific stylistic and topical preferences and exert a causal influence on generation. We introduce Differential Preference Steering (DPS), a training free framework that (1) identifies Preference Heads through causal masking analysis and (2) leverages them for controllable and interpretable personalization at inference time. DPS computes a Preference Contribution Score (PCS) for each attention head, directly measuring its causal impact on user aligned outputs. During decoding, we contrast model predictions with and without Preference Heads, amplifying the difference between personalized and generic logits to selectively strengthen preference aligned continuations. Experiments on widely used personalization benchmarks across multiple LLMs demonstrate consistent gains in personalization fidelity while preserving content coherence and low computational overhead. Beyond empirical improvements, DPS provides a mechanistic explanation of where and how personalization emerges within transformer architectures. Our implementation is publicly available.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies and manipulates specific attention heads in LLMs that encode user preferences, enabling training-free, interpretable personalization.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM personalization methods treat models as black boxes and rely on prompt engineering or fine-tuning, which can be inefficient, opaque, and hard to control. The authors want a mechanistic, interpretable, and training-free way to understand and steer how personalization arises inside transformer architectures.
AI.method: They propose Differential Preference Steering (DPS), which first uses causal masking analysis to find "Preference Heads"—attention heads whose activations causally influence user-specific stylistic and topical outputs. They define a Preference Contribution Score (PCS) to quantify each head’s causal effect on preference-aligned text. At inference time, they compare model predictions with and without these heads and modify the logits to amplify differences that correspond to user-aligned preferences, thus steering generation in a controllable way without additional training.
AI.result: Across multiple LLMs and standard personalization benchmarks, DPS improves personalization fidelity while maintaining coherence. The approach incurs low computational overhead and works consistently across settings, showing that manipulating identified Preference Heads yields better alignment with user preferences than baselines that don’t use this mechanistic structure.
AI.conclusion: Personalization in LLMs is partly localized in a sparse set of attention heads that encode user preferences. By identifying and steering these Preference Heads, DPS offers a training-free, interpretable, and effective method for controllable personalization, and sheds light on the internal mechanisms by which transformers implement personalization.

title: Selective Contrastive Learning For Gloss Free Sign Language Translation
comment: Accepted by ACL 2026 as the main conference
summary: Sign language translation (SLT) converts continuous sign videos into spoken-language text, yet it remains challenging due to the intrinsic modality mismatch between visual signs and written text, particularly in gloss-free settings. Recent SLT systems increasingly adopt CLIP-like Vision-Language pretraining (VLP) for cross-modal alignment, but the random in-batch contrast provides few, batch-dependent negatives and may mislabel semantically similar (or even identical) pairs as negatives, introducing noisy and potentially inconsistent alignment supervision. In this work, we first conduct a preliminary trajectory-based analysis that tracks negative video-text similarity over training. The results show that only a small subset of negatives exhibits the desired behavior of being consistently pushed away, while the remaining negatives display heterogeneous and often non-decreasing similarity dynamics, suggesting that random in-batch negatives are frequently uninformative for effective alignment. Inspired by this, we propose Selective Contrastive Learning for SLT (SCL-SLT) with a Pair Selection (PS) strategy. PS scores candidate negatives using similarity dynamics from reference checkpoints and constructs mini-batches via a curriculum that progressively emphasizes more challenging negatives, thereby strengthening contrastive supervision while reducing the influence of noisy or semantically invalid negatives.

AI.tldr: They improve sign language translation by selecting more informative negative pairs during contrastive vision-language pretraining, instead of using all random in-batch negatives.
AI.motivation: Sign language translation is difficult because videos and text are very different modalities, and current CLIP-like pretraining relies on random in-batch negatives that can be few, uninformative, or even semantically wrong, leading to noisy cross-modal alignment supervision.
AI.method: They first analyze how similarity between videos and negative texts evolves during training using trajectory-based analysis, finding that many random negatives are not effectively pushed away. Based on this, they introduce Selective Contrastive Learning for SLT (SCL-SLT), which includes a Pair Selection (PS) strategy that scores candidate negatives using similarity dynamics from reference checkpoints and constructs a curriculum of mini-batches that increasingly focus on harder but valid negatives.
AI.result: The analysis shows that only a small subset of negatives behaves as desired (consistently decreasing similarity), while many exhibit flat or increasing similarity, revealing that random negatives are often uninformative. The proposed SCL-SLT with PS strengthens contrastive supervision by selecting more challenging and reliable negatives and reducing the impact of noisy or semantically invalid ones, improving cross-modal alignment for SLT.
AI.conclusion: Random in-batch negatives in CLIP-like SLT pretraining are frequently weak or misleading for learning good video-text alignment. By selectively choosing negatives based on their similarity trajectories and introducing a curriculum that focuses on increasingly harder, valid negatives, SCL-SLT provides more effective contrastive learning and leads to better sign language translation performance.

title: Measuring and Mitigating Persona Distortions from AI Writing Assistance
comment: For supplementary information, code, and data see https://github.com/paul-rottger/ai-distortion
summary: Hundreds of millions of people use artificial intelligence (AI) for writing assistance. Here, we evaluated how AI writing assistance distorts writer personas - their perceived beliefs, personality, and identity. In three large-scale experiments, writers (N=2,939) wrote political opinion paragraphs with and without AI assistance. Separate groups of readers (N=11,091) blindly evaluated these paragraphs across 29 socially salient dimensions of reader perception, spanning political opinion, writing quality, writer personality, emotions, and demographics. AI writing assistance produced persona distortions across all dimensions: with AI, writers seemed more opinionated, competent, and positive, and their perceived demographic profile shifted towards more privileged groups. Writers objected to many of the observed distortions, yet continued to prefer AI-assisted text even when made aware of them. We successfully mitigated objectionable persona distortions at the model level by training reward models on our experimental data (10,008 paragraphs, 2,903,596 ratings) to steer AI outputs towards faithful representation of writer stance. However, this came at a cost to user acceptance, suggesting an entanglement between desirable and undesirable properties of AI writing assistance that may be difficult to resolve. Together, our findings demonstrate that persona distortions from AI writing assistance are pervasive and persistent even under realistic conditions of human oversight, which carries implications for public discourse, trust, and democratic deliberation that scale with AI adoption.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how AI writing assistance changes how readers perceive the writer’s persona and whether these shifts can be reduced without losing user satisfaction.
AI.motivation: AI writing tools are widely used, but it is unclear how they alter perceptions of the author’s beliefs, personality, and identity, especially in sensitive domains like political opinion. The authors want to understand whether AI assistance systematically distorts writer personas in ways that may affect public discourse and democratic processes.
AI.method: The authors ran three large-scale experiments where nearly 3,000 writers produced political opinion paragraphs with and without AI assistance. Over 11,000 readers, blind to condition, rated these texts along 29 dimensions covering political stance, writing quality, personality traits, emotions, and demographic attributes. They quantified ‘persona distortion’ as shifts in reader perceptions between assisted and unassisted texts. Then they trained reward models on a large dataset of paragraphs and reader ratings to steer AI outputs toward better reflecting the writer’s original stance and evaluated the impact on both persona distortion and user acceptance.
AI.result: AI assistance systematically changed perceived writer personas across all measured dimensions, making writers appear more opinionated, more competent, more positive, and demographically closer to privileged groups. Writers often disagreed with these distortions but still preferred the AI-assisted versions even when informed. Reward-model-based steering reduced objectionable persona distortions but also reduced user acceptance of the AI-generated text, indicating a trade-off between fidelity to writer persona and perceived quality or desirability of assistance.
AI.conclusion: AI writing assistance pervasively and persistently distorts perceived writer personas, even with human oversight. Attempts to correct these distortions at the model level face an inherent trade-off with user satisfaction, suggesting that desirable attributes (e.g., polished, positive writing) are entangled with undesirable shifts in perceived identity and stance. These findings imply that widespread AI-assisted writing can subtly reshape public discourse, trust, and democratic deliberation as adoption grows.

title: Aggregate vs. Personalized Judges in Business Idea Evaluation: Evidence from Expert Disagreement
summary: Evaluating LLM-generated business ideas is often harder to scale than generating them. Unlike standard NLP benchmarks, business idea evaluation relies on multi-dimensional criteria such as feasibility, novelty, differentiation, user need, and market size, and expert judgments often disagree. This paper studies a methodological question raised by such disagreement: should an automatic judge approximate an aggregate consensus, or model evaluators individually? We introduce PBIG-DATA, a dataset of approximately 3,000 individual scores across 300 patent-grounded product ideas, provided by domain experts on six business-oriented dimensions: specificity, technical validity, innovativeness, competitive advantage, need validity, and market size. Analyses show substantial expert disagreement on fine-grained ordinal scores, while agreement is higher under coarse selection, suggesting structured heterogeneity rather than random noise. We then compare three judge configurations: a rubric-only zero-shot judge, an aggregate judge conditioned on mixed evaluator histories, and a personalized judge conditioned on the target evaluator's scoring history. Across dimensions and model sizes, personalized judges align more closely with the corresponding evaluator than aggregate judges, and evaluator agreement correlates with similarity of judge-generated reasoning only under personalized conditioning. These results indicate that pooled labels can be a fragile target in pluralistic evaluation settings and motivate evaluator-conditioned judge designs for business idea assessment.

AI.tldr: The paper builds and analyzes a dataset of expert evaluations of LLM-generated, patent-grounded business ideas, and shows that automatic judges work better when personalized to individual evaluators rather than trained on pooled labels.
AI.motivation: Evaluating LLM-generated business ideas is complex and not easily scalable, because it must account for multiple business dimensions (feasibility, novelty, market, etc.) and experts often disagree in structured ways. This raises a methodological question: should automatic evaluation systems aim to match a consensus label or model individual evaluators’ preferences and criteria?
AI.method: The authors create PBIG-DATA, a dataset with about 3,000 individual ratings on 300 patent-grounded product ideas, each evaluated by domain experts along six business-specific dimensions. They analyze the extent and structure of expert disagreement, then implement and compare three types of LLM-based judges: (1) a rubric-only zero-shot judge, (2) an aggregate judge conditioned on mixed evaluator histories, and (3) a personalized judge conditioned on each target evaluator’s past scoring history. They measure alignment between each judge’s scores and the human evaluators’ scores and reasoning.
AI.result: Expert judgments show substantial disagreement on detailed ordinal scores but higher agreement for coarse-grained selection decisions, indicating systematic heterogeneity rather than random noise. Across multiple business dimensions and model sizes, personalized judges more accurately match their corresponding evaluator’s scores than aggregate judges do. Furthermore, similarity in judge-generated reasoning correlates with evaluator agreement only when the judge is personalized to the evaluator.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that in pluralistic, multi-criteria settings like business idea evaluation, pooling labels to form a single consensus target can be fragile and potentially misleading. Instead, conditioning automatic judges on individual evaluator histories—i.e., personalizing the judge—yields better alignment with human experts and offers a more faithful way to model heterogeneous expert perspectives for assessing LLM-generated business ideas.

title: RouteLMT: Learned Sample Routing for Hybrid LLM Translation Deployment
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have achieved remarkable performance in Machine Translation (MT), but deploying them at scale remains prohibitively expensive. A widely adopted remedy is the hybrid system paradigm, which balances cost and quality by serving most requests with a small model and selectively routing a fraction to a large model. However, existing routing strategies often rely on heuristics, external predictors, or absolute quality estimation, which fail to capture whether the large model actually provides a worthwhile improvement over the small one. In this paper, we formulate routing as a budget allocation problem and identify marginal gain, i.e., the large model's improvement over the small model, as the optimal signal for budgeted decisions. Building on this, we propose \textbf{RouteLMT} (routing for LLM-based MT), an efficient in-model router that predicts this expected gain by probing the small translators prompt-token representation, without requiring external models or hypothesis decoding. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our RouteLMT outperforms heuristics, quality/difficulty estimation baselines, achieving a superior quality-budget Pareto frontier. Furthermore, we analyze regression risks and show that a simple guarded variant can mitigate severe quality losses.

AI.tldr: They propose RouteLMT, an in-model routing method that decides when to use a large MT LLM by directly predicting its marginal quality gain over a smaller model, improving the quality-cost tradeoff.
AI.motivation: LLM-based machine translation is strong but too expensive to use for all requests. Existing hybrid systems that mix small and large models don’t optimally decide which requests to send to the large model because they rely on rough heuristics or quality estimates that don’t reflect the *improvement* a large model would bring over a small one. A principled, budget-aware routing approach is needed.
AI.method: They cast routing as a budget allocation problem and identify the theoretically optimal decision signal as the marginal gain—the large model’s expected improvement over the small model on each input. They then design RouteLMT, an in-model router that probes the small model’s internal prompt-token representations to predict this marginal gain, without running the large model or decoding candidate translations. The router is trained as a regression model over these internal features to estimate the expected benefit of switching to the large model, and is integrated into the small MT model so routing can be done efficiently at inference time.
AI.result: RouteLMT consistently beats heuristic routing and standard quality/difficulty estimators across various MT settings, yielding a better Pareto frontier between translation quality and computational budget. Experiments further show that the proposed approach remains efficient since it does not require external predictors or decoding. They also empirically study cases where regression-based gain prediction can cause severe quality drops and characterize these failure modes.
AI.conclusion: Using marginal gain as the routing signal and predicting it directly from the small model’s internal representations enables more principled and effective budgeted routing between small and large MT LLMs. RouteLMT offers a superior cost-quality tradeoff versus existing methods, and a simple guarded variant can alleviate extreme regressions, making the method practical for real-world deployment.

title: Using Embedding Models to Improve Probabilistic Race Prediction
summary: Estimating racial disparity requires individual-level race data, which are often unavailable due to the sensitivity of collecting such information. To address this problem, many researchers utilize Bayesian Improved Surname Geocoding (BISG), which have critically relied on Census surname data. Unfortunately, these data capture race-surname relationships only for common surnames, omitting approximately 10% of the US population. We show that predictive performance degrades substantially for individuals with such omitted, uncommon surnames because standard BISG implementation relies on a uninformative generic prior in these cases. To address this limitation, we propose embedding-powered BISG (eBISG), which uses pre-trained text embeddings to represent names as dense vectors and trains neural networks on 2020 Census surname and first-name data to estimate race probabilities for names not covered in the Census. We compare five approaches: standard BISG using only surnames, BIFSG incorporating first name probabilities, surname embedding for unlisted names, surname and first name embedding combining both, and a full-name embedding trained on voter file data from Southern states that captures interactions between name components. We show that each successive eBISG approach improves race prediction, with the full-name embedding yielding the largest gains, particularly for Hispanic and Asian voters whose surnames are absent from the Census list.

AI.tldr: They improve race prediction for individuals with uncommon surnames by using name embeddings and neural networks instead of the generic prior in standard BISG.
AI.motivation: Standard Bayesian Improved Surname Geocoding (BISG) relies on Census surname data, which only cover common surnames and omit about 10% of the US population. For people with these uncommon surnames, existing BISG methods default to an uninformative prior, substantially degrading predictive performance in estimating racial disparities. There is a need for a method that can produce accurate race probabilities for individuals whose names are not in the Census surname list, without directly collecting sensitive race information.
AI.method: They introduce embedding-powered BISG (eBISG), which represents names as dense vectors using pre-trained text embeddings and then trains neural networks on 2020 Census surname and first-name data to predict race probabilities. They evaluate five methods: (1) standard BISG using only surnames, (2) BIFSG adding first-name-based probabilities, (3) an embedding-based model for surnames that are not listed, (4) a combined surname and first-name embedding model, and (5) a full-name embedding model trained on Southern voter files that captures interactions between first and last names. Performance across these methods is compared on race prediction tasks, particularly for individuals with surnames missing from the Census list.
AI.result: Predictive accuracy degrades sharply for individuals whose surnames are absent from the Census data when using standard BISG because it defaults to a generic, uninformative prior. The proposed embedding-powered approaches systematically improve race prediction over this baseline. Each enhancement—adding first names, using embeddings for missing surnames, combining first and last name embeddings, and finally applying a full-name embedding trained on voter file data—yields incremental gains. The full-name embedding approach provides the largest performance improvements, especially for Hispanic and Asian voters with non-Census-listed surnames.
AI.conclusion: Embedding-powered BISG (eBISG) can substantially mitigate the limitations of standard BISG for individuals with uncommon or unlisted surnames. By leveraging text embeddings and neural networks trained on Census and voter file data, eBISG provides more accurate race probability estimates without requiring direct racial self-identification. This is particularly beneficial for groups, such as Hispanic and Asian voters, who are disproportionately represented among those with surnames missing from the Census list, thereby improving the quality and fairness of racial disparity estimation in administrative and research datasets.

title: Dharma, Data and Deception: An LLM-Powered Rhetorical Analysis of Cow-Urine Health Claims on YouTube
summary: Health misinformation remains one of the most pressing challenges on social media, particularly when cultural traditions intersect with scientific-sounding claims. These dynamics are not only global but also deeply local, manifesting in culturally specific controversies that require careful analysis. Motivated by this, we examine 100 YouTube transcripts that promote or debunk cow urine (gomutra) as a health remedy, focusing on rhetorical strategies such as appeals to authority, efficacy appeals, and conspiracy framing. We employ large language models (LLMs) including GPT-4, GPT-4o, GPT-4.1, GPT-5, Gemini 2.5 Pro, and Mistral Medium 3 to annotate transcripts using a 14-category taxonomy of persuasive tactics. Our analysis reveals that promoters predominantly rely on efficacy appeals and social proof, while debunkers emphasize authority and rebuttal. Human evaluation of a subset of annotations yielded 90.1\% inter-annotator agreement, confirming the reliability of our taxonomy and validation process. This work advances computational methods for misinformation analysis and demonstrates how LLMs can support large-scale studies of cultural discourse online.

AI.tldr: The paper uses large language models to analyze how YouTube videos promote or debunk cow urine as a health remedy, identifying distinct persuasive tactics used by each side.
AI.motivation: Health misinformation on social media is a serious problem, especially when it draws legitimacy from local cultural traditions and mixes them with scientific-sounding rhetoric. Existing work often overlooks how these dynamics play out in specific cultural contexts or at scale. The authors aim to understand the persuasive strategies used in a culturally specific controversy—cow urine as a health remedy—and to test whether LLMs can reliably annotate such rhetorical tactics for large-scale analysis.
AI.method: The authors collect 100 YouTube video transcripts that either promote or debunk the use of cow urine as a health remedy. They define a 14-category taxonomy of persuasive tactics, including appeals to authority, efficacy appeals, social proof, conspiracy framing, and rebuttal. Multiple large language models (GPT-4, GPT-4o, GPT-4.1, GPT-5, Gemini 2.5 Pro, Mistral Medium 3) are used as annotators to label the presence of these tactics in each transcript. A subset of the LLM-generated annotations is then evaluated by human annotators to assess reliability and agreement with the taxonomy.
AI.result: The analysis shows clear rhetorical differences between promoters and debunkers. Promoter videos rely heavily on efficacy appeals (claims that the remedy works, often with anecdotal or unverified support) and social proof (references to community use, tradition, or popularity). Debunker videos instead foreground appeals to authority (scientific expertise, medical consensus) and employ rebuttal strategies to counter misinformation. Human evaluation of a subset of LLM annotations yields a high inter-annotator agreement of 90.1%, indicating that both the taxonomy and the LLM-assisted annotation process are reliable.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that culturally rooted health misinformation can be systematically analyzed through a taxonomy of persuasive strategies, and that LLMs are effective tools for scaling such annotation tasks. The distinct rhetorical profiles of promoters versus debunkers suggest targeted ways to understand and address misinformation. Overall, the work contributes to computational misinformation research and shows how LLM-based annotation can support large-scale studies of cultural discourse on platforms like YouTube.

title: From graphemic dependence to lexical structure: a Markovian perspective on Dante's Commedia
comment: 25 pages, 8 figures, 1 supplementary material; submitted to Digital Scholarship in the Humanities
summary:
This study investigates the structural organisation of Dante's Divina Commedia through a symbolic representation based on vowel-consonant (V/C) encoding. Modelling the resulting sequence as a four-state Markov chain yields a parsimonious index of graphemic memory, capturing the balance between persistence and alternation patterns.
  Across the poem, this index exhibits a slight but consistent increase from the Inferno to the Paradiso, indicating a directional shift in local dependency structure. Trigram-level analysis shows that this trend is driven by a restricted set of recurrent configurations, interpreted as graphemic probes linking the Markov representation to identifiable lexical environments in the text.
  These probes display distinct behaviours: configurations involving two transitions more frequently emerge across word boundaries, reflecting interactions between adjacent tokens, whereas configurations with fewer transitions are largely confined to intra-lexical structures. Part of the signal is further shaped by orthographic phenomena, particularly apostrophised forms, highlighting the role of writing conventions alongside phonological and lexical organisation.
  A complementary classification analysis identifies cantica-specific terms, providing lexical anchors through which graphemic probes can be related to the structure of the poem. This organisation is reflected not only in the separation of the three cantiche, but also in a continuous trajectory across the text.
  Overall, the results show that simple probabilistic models applied to symbolic text representations can uncover structured interactions between local dependencies, lexical distribution, orthographic encoding, and large-scale organisation, providing an interpretable framework for linking local symbolic dynamics to higher-level textual organisation.

AI.tldr: The paper models Dante’s Divina Commedia using a vowel/consonant encoding and Markov chains to reveal how local letter patterns relate to the poem’s large-scale organisation from Inferno to Paradiso.
AI.motivation: To understand whether very simple, low-level symbolic features of text (vowel/consonant patterns) and basic probabilistic models (Markov chains) can reveal systematic structural differences and organisation across Dante’s three cantiche, linking micro-level textual dynamics to macro-level poetic structure.
AI.method: The authors encode the text of the Divina Commedia as sequences of vowels and consonants and model these sequences with a four-state Markov chain. They derive an index of graphemic memory from the balance of persistence vs. alternation in this chain. They then examine how this index changes from Inferno through Purgatorio to Paradiso. They perform trigram-level analyses to identify recurrent V/C configurations (“graphemic probes”) and study whether they occur across or within word boundaries, and how they relate to orthographic conventions (e.g. apostrophised forms). Finally, they run a classification analysis to identify cantica-specific lexical items and connect these lexical anchors to the graphemic probes and the overall trajectory of the poem.
AI.result: The graphemic memory index slightly but consistently increases from Inferno to Paradiso, indicating changing local dependency patterns across the poem. A small, restricted set of V/C trigrams accounts for this trend and can be tied to specific lexical environments. Probes with two transitions tend to span word boundaries, reflecting inter-word interactions, while those with fewer transitions mainly occur within words. Orthographic features, notably apostrophised forms, significantly affect the signal. Classification analysis identifies terms characteristic of each cantica and shows that the text follows a continuous structural trajectory, not just a coarse three-way separation.
AI.conclusion: Simple probabilistic models over low-level symbolic encodings can reveal meaningful structure in a complex literary text. In the Divina Commedia, vowel/consonant Markov modelling uncovers systematic changes in local dependency patterns from Inferno to Paradiso, shaped by both lexical distribution and orthographic conventions, and aligned with the poem’s large-scale division and progression. This provides an interpretable bridge between local symbolic dynamics and high-level textual organisation.

title: Identifying and typifying demographic unfairness in phoneme-level embeddings of self-supervised speech recognition models
summary: Modern automatic speech recognition (ASR) systems have been observed to function better for certain speaker groups (SGs) than others, despite recent gains in overall performance. One potential impediment to progress towards fairer ASR is a more nuanced understanding of the types of modeling errors that speech encoder models make, and in particular the difference between the structure of embeddings for high-performance and low-performance SGs. This paper proposes a framework typifying two types of error that can occur in modeling phonemes in ASR systems: random error/high variance in phoneme embedding, vs systematic error/embedding bias. We find that training phoneme classification probes only on a single, typically disadvantaged SG, sometimes improves performance for that SG, which is evidence for the existence of SG-level bias in phoneme embeddings. On the other hand, we find that speakers and SGs with higher levels of phoneme variance are the same as those with worse phoneme prediction accuracy. We conclude that both types of error are present in phoneme embeddings and both are candidate causes for SG-level unfairness in ASR, though random error is likely a greater hindrance to fairness than systematic error. Furthermore, we find that finetuning encoder models using a fairness-enhancing algorithm (domain enhancing and adversarial training) changes neither the benefits of in-domain phoneme classification probe training, nor measured levels of random embedding error.

AI.tldr: The paper studies why ASR systems perform differently across speaker groups by analyzing phoneme embedding errors, distinguishing between random variance and systematic bias, and finds random error is the larger obstacle to fairness.
AI.motivation: Although ASR systems have improved overall, they still perform worse for some speaker groups. To make ASR fairer, we need a clearer understanding of what kinds of representation errors speech encoders make for different groups, especially at the phoneme level.
AI.method: The authors propose a framework that categorizes phoneme modeling errors into random error (high variance in phoneme embeddings) and systematic error (embedding bias). They train phoneme classification probes on embeddings, including probes trained only on a single, typically disadvantaged speaker group, and analyze the performance and variance characteristics across speakers and groups. They also evaluate the effect of finetuning encoders with a fairness-focused algorithm combining domain enhancement and adversarial training.
AI.result: Probes trained only on a disadvantaged speaker group sometimes improve performance for that group, indicating speaker-group-level bias in phoneme embeddings. Speaker groups with higher phoneme embedding variance also exhibit worse phoneme prediction accuracy, linking random error to poorer performance. Finetuning the encoder with a fairness-enhancing algorithm does not materially change the benefits from in-domain probe training or the measured levels of random embedding error.
AI.conclusion: Both systematic error (bias) and random error (variance) exist in phoneme embeddings and contribute to unfairness across speaker groups in ASR, but random error appears to be the larger barrier to fairness. Additionally, current fairness-oriented finetuning (via domain enhancement and adversarial training) does not effectively reduce random embedding error or alter the observed error structure across groups.

title: BERAG: Bayesian Ensemble Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-based Visual Question Answering
summary: A common approach to question answering with retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) is to concatenate documents into a single context and pass it to a language model to generate an answer. While simple, this strategy can obscure the contribution of individual documents, making attribution difficult and contributing to the ``lost-in-the-middle'' effect, where relevant information in long contexts is overlooked. Concatenation also scales poorly: computational cost grows quadratically with context length, a problem that becomes especially severe when the context includes visual data, as in visual question answering. Attempts to mitigate these issues by limiting context length can further restrict performance by preventing models from benefiting from the improved recall offered by deeper retrieval. We propose Bayesian Ensemble Retrieval-Augmented Generation (BERAG), along with Bayesian Ensemble Fine-Tuning (BEFT), as a RAG framework in which language models are conditioned on individual retrieved documents rather than a single combined context. BERAG treats document posterior probabilities as ensemble weights and updates them token by token using Bayes' rule during generation. This approach enables probabilistic re-ranking, parallel memory usage, and clear attribution of document contribution, making it well-suited for large document collections. We evaluate BERAG and BEFT primarily on knowledge-based visual question answering tasks, where models must reason over long, imperfect retrieval lists. The results show substantial improvements over standard RAG, including strong gains on Document Visual Question Answering and multimodal needle-in-a-haystack benchmarks. We also demonstrate that BERAG mitigates the ``lost-in-the-middle'' effect. The document posterior can be used to detect insufficient grounding and trigger deflection, while document pruning enables faster decoding than standard RAG.

AI.tldr: Proposes BERAG and BEFT, a Bayesian ensemble framework for RAG that conditions on documents individually, updates their importance during generation, and improves performance and efficiency, especially for long-context visual QA.
AI.motivation: Standard RAG concatenates many retrieved documents into one long context, which hides each document’s contribution, causes lost-in-the-middle failures on long inputs, scales quadratically with context length (worse with visual features), and forces context truncation that wastes deep retrieval. There is a need for a method that scales better, preserves attribution, and can exploit long, noisy retrieval lists.
AI.method: Introduce Bayesian Ensemble RAG (BERAG), where the model is run separately (conceptually or in parallel) conditioned on individual retrieved documents instead of a merged context. Maintain a posterior probability over documents that acts as ensemble weights, updated token by token using Bayes’ rule based on the partial generation. Extend this with Bayesian Ensemble Fine-Tuning (BEFT) so the LM learns to work under this ensemble setting. The framework supports probabilistic re-ranking of documents, parallel use of memory, and pruning of low-probability documents during decoding.
AI.result: On knowledge-based visual question answering benchmarks with long, imperfect retrieval lists—such as Document Visual Question Answering and multimodal needle-in-a-haystack tasks—BERAG and BEFT substantially outperform standard concatenation-based RAG. They show better robustness to long contexts, improved accuracy, mitigation of the lost-in-the-middle effect, and faster decoding when using posterior-based pruning, while maintaining or improving grounding quality.
AI.conclusion: Conditioning on documents individually and combining them via a Bayesian ensemble during generation is a more effective and scalable alternative to concatenation-based RAG, particularly for long and multimodal contexts. BERAG provides clearer attribution, mitigates lost-in-the-middle, supports grounding detection and deflection via document posteriors, and can even be more efficient through document pruning, making it well-suited for large document collections in visual QA and similar tasks.

title: Thinking Without Words: Efficient Latent Reasoning with Abstract Chain-of-Thought
summary: While long, explicit chains-of-thought (CoT) have proven effective on complex reasoning tasks, they are costly to generate during inference. Non-verbal reasoning methods have emerged with shorter generation lengths by leveraging continuous representations, yet their performance lags behind verbalized CoT. We propose $\textbf{Abstract Chain-of-Thought}$, a discrete latent reasoning post-training mechanism in which the language model produces a short sequence of tokens from a reserved vocabulary in lieu of a natural language CoT, before generating a response. To make previously unseen ''abstract'' tokens useful, we introduce a policy iteration-style warm-up loop that alternates between (i.) bottlenecking from a verbal CoT via masking and performing supervised fine-tuning, and (ii.) self-distillation by training the model to generate abstract tokens from the prompt alone via constrained decoding with the codebook. After warm-up, we optimize the generation of abstract sequences with warm-started reinforcement learning under constrained decoding. Abstract-CoT achieves up to $11.6\times$ fewer reasoning tokens while demonstrating comparable performance across mathematical reasoning, instruction-following, and multi-hop reasoning, and generalizes across language model families. We also find an emergent power law distribution over the abstract vocabulary, akin to those seen in natural language, that evolves across the training phases. Our findings highlight the potential for post-training latent reasoning mechanisms that enable efficient inference through a learned abstract reasoning language.

AI.tldr: They introduce Abstract Chain-of-Thought: short, learned discrete “reasoning codes” that replace long natural-language chains-of-thought, preserving accuracy while greatly reducing reasoning token length.
AI.motivation: Explicit natural-language chain-of-thought improves complex reasoning but is expensive at inference because it requires generating many tokens. New non-verbal/continuous reasoning methods shorten outputs but still underperform verbal CoT. The authors want a method that keeps CoT-level reasoning performance while cutting token cost and remaining compatible with standard language models.
AI.method: They define a reserved, small vocabulary of special “abstract” tokens that act as a latent reasoning language. During inference the model first generates a short sequence of these abstract tokens, then produces the final answer conditioned on them. To make these new tokens useful, they use a multi-phase post-training procedure: (1) Warm-up with a policy-iteration style loop: (i) bottleneck learning—mask and compress existing verbal CoTs into short abstract sequences and fine-tune with supervised learning so the model learns to map rich CoTs through the abstract vocabulary; (ii) self-distillation—train the model to generate suitable abstract tokens directly from the prompt using constrained decoding over the abstract codebook. (2) After warm-up, apply reinforcement learning, again with constrained decoding, to further optimize the abstract-token sequences for task performance. The process is applied as a post-training mechanism on different base LMs.
AI.result: Abstract-CoT reduces the number of explicit reasoning tokens by up to 11.6× compared to natural language CoT while maintaining comparable accuracy on mathematical reasoning, instruction-following, and multi-hop reasoning benchmarks. The method transfers across different language model families. They further observe that usage frequencies of abstract tokens follow a power-law distribution, similar to natural language word frequencies, and this distribution evolves over training phases.
AI.conclusion: A discrete, learned abstract reasoning language can serve as an efficient latent chain-of-thought: it retains most of the performance benefits of explicit CoT while drastically cutting reasoning token length and inference cost. Post-training mechanisms combining supervised bottlenecking, self-distillation, and RL can successfully induce such a codebook, and the emergent power-law structure suggests that the learned abstract tokens function analogously to words in natural language. This opens a path to scalable, efficient reasoning in LMs using compact latent representations instead of verbose verbal explanations.

title: Representational Harms in LLM-Generated Narratives Against Global Majority Nationalities
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used for text generation tasks from everyday use to high-stakes enterprise and government applications, including simulated interviews with asylum seekers. While many works highlight the new potential applications of LLMs, there are risks of LLMs encoding and perpetuating harmful biases about non-dominant communities across the globe. To better evaluate and mitigate such harms, more research examining how LLMs portray diverse individuals is needed. In this work, we study how national origin identities are portrayed by widely-adopted LLMs in response to open-ended narrative generation prompts. Our findings demonstrate the presence of persistent representational harms by national origin, including harmful stereotypes, erasure, and one-dimensional portrayals of Global Majority identities. Minoritized national identities are simultaneously underrepresented in power-neutral stories and overrepresented in subordinated character portrayals, which are over fifty times more likely to appear than dominant portrayals. The degree of harm is amplified when US nationality cues (e.g., ``American'') are present in input prompts. Notably, we find that the harms we identify cannot be explained away via sycophancy, as US-centric biases persist even when replacing US nationality cues with non-US national identities in the prompts. Based on our findings, we call for further exploration of cultural harms in LLMs through methodologies that center Global Majority perspectives and challenge the uncritical adoption of US-based LLMs for the classification, surveillance, and misrepresentation of the majority of our planet.

AI.tldr: The paper examines how large language models represent different national origin identities and finds pervasive, harmful, and US-centric biases in their narrative generations.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly deployed in sensitive real-world contexts, there is a growing concern that they may encode and reproduce harmful biases, especially toward non-dominant and Global Majority communities. Existing work has focused more on showcasing applications than on deeply examining how these models portray diverse individuals in open-ended, narrative settings. The authors aim to fill this gap to better evaluate and mitigate cultural and representational harms.
AI.method: The authors systematically probe widely used LLMs with open-ended narrative generation prompts that specify different national origin identities, including Global Majority and minoritized groups. They analyze the generated stories for patterns of representation, such as stereotypes, erasure, and the power status of characters (dominant vs subordinated roles). They also manipulate prompts to include or exclude US nationality cues like "American" to study how such cues influence representational harms and whether effects can be attributed to sycophancy.
AI.result: The study finds persistent representational harms based on national origin. Global Majority and other minoritized national identities are subject to harmful stereotypes, erasure, and one-dimensional portrayals. These identities appear less often in neutral or power-balanced stories but are disproportionately cast in subordinated roles, which occur over fifty times more frequently than dominant portrayals. The harms become more severe when prompts contain US nationality cues, and US-centric biases remain even when US cues are replaced with non-US nationalities, indicating that sycophancy does not fully explain the behavior.
AI.conclusion: LLMs, as currently deployed, exhibit systematic and harmful biases in how they portray different national origin identities, especially those from the Global Majority. These harms are structurally tied to US-centric training and deployment contexts and cannot be dismissed as simple sycophancy. The authors argue that future research on LLM evaluation and mitigation must explicitly center Global Majority perspectives and critically scrutinize the adoption of US-based LLMs in tasks involving classification, surveillance, and representation of people around the world.

title: How Do AI Agents Spend Your Money? Analyzing and Predicting Token Consumption in Agentic Coding Tasks
summary: The wide adoption of AI agents in complex human workflows is driving rapid growth in LLM token consumption. When agents are deployed on tasks that require a significant amount of tokens, three questions naturally arise: (1) Where do AI agents spend the tokens? (2) Which models are more token-efficient? and (3) Can agents predict their token usage before task execution? In this paper, we present the first systematic study of token consumption patterns in agentic coding tasks. We analyze trajectories from eight frontier LLMs on SWE-bench Verified and evaluate models' ability to predict their own token costs before task execution. We find that: (1) agentic tasks are uniquely expensive, consuming 1000x more tokens than code reasoning and code chat, with input tokens rather than output tokens driving the overall cost; (2) token usage is highly variable and inherently stochastic: runs on the same task can differ by up to 30x in total tokens, and higher token usage does not translate into higher accuracy; instead, accuracy often peaks at intermediate cost and saturates at higher costs; (3) models vary substantially in token efficiency: on the same tasks, Kimi-K2 and Claude-Sonnet-4.5, on average, consume over 1.5 million more tokens than GPT-5; (4) task difficulty rated by human experts only weakly aligns with actual token costs, revealing a fundamental gap between human-perceived complexity and the computational effort agents actually expend; and (5) frontier models fail to accurately predict their own token usage (with weak-to-moderate correlations, up to 0.39) and systematically underestimate real token costs. Our study offers new insights into the economics of AI agents and can inspire future research in this direction.

AI.tldr: The paper systematically studies where and how AI coding agents consume tokens, how efficient different frontier LLMs are, and whether they can predict their own token usage, revealing huge, stochastic, and often inefficient token consumption that models themselves poorly anticipate.
AI.motivation: AI agents used in complex coding workflows consume enormous numbers of LLM tokens, which has economic and practical implications. However, we lack a clear, empirical understanding of (1) where tokens are actually spent in these agentic pipelines, (2) which models are more token-efficient for such tasks, and (3) whether agents can estimate their costs in advance to enable budgeting and planning. This gap prevents informed deployment, optimization, and pricing of agentic systems.
AI.method: The authors run agentic coding workflows on SWE-bench Verified using eight frontier LLMs. They log full trajectories, including all intermediate calls, to precisely measure token consumption patterns. They then (a) compare agentic coding token usage to simpler code reasoning/chat tasks, (b) analyze variability and stochasticity in token usage across repeated runs on the same tasks, (c) compare models’ token efficiency on identical tasks, (d) correlate human expert difficulty ratings with actual token costs, and (e) prompt models to predict their own token usage before execution and correlate these predictions with realized costs.
AI.result: They find: (1) agentic coding tasks consume up to 1000x more tokens than standard code reasoning/chat, driven mainly by input tokens; (2) token usage is highly variable and stochastic, differing by up to 30x across runs on the same task, with more tokens not reliably improving accuracy—performance often peaks at intermediate costs; (3) different models show large gaps in token efficiency, with Kimi-K2 and Claude-Sonnet-4.5 using on average >1.5M more tokens than GPT-5 on the same tasks; (4) human-rated task difficulty only weakly correlates with actual token cost, indicating a mismatch between human-perceived complexity and computational effort; and (5) frontier models are poor at predicting their own token usage in advance, showing only weak-to-moderate correlation (up to 0.39) and a tendency to underestimate costs.
AI.conclusion: Agentic coding workflows are extraordinarily token-hungry, with costs dominated by inputs, large stochastic variability, and substantial inefficiencies across models. More tokens do not guarantee better accuracy, and expert judgments of difficulty do not reliably track computational cost. Current frontier LLMs cannot accurately forecast their own token usage, systematically underestimating it. These findings highlight the need for better tools, architectures, and cost-aware strategies to manage and optimize the economics of AI agents.
